responseSet.documentInfoXML: 2003 84 1 169-172 The Castaway Self: A Psychoanalytic Reading of Castaway (2000) Director: Robert Zemeckis Paola Golinelli Via San Pietro 29, 35139 Padova, Italy Paola.golinelli@inwind.it
responseSet.title: The Castaway Self: A Psychoanalytic Reading of Castaway (2000): Director: Robert Zemeckis
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Castaway Self: A Psychoanalytic Reading of Castaway (2000)

Director: Robert Zemeckis

Paola Golinelli

Paola Golinelli

Via San Pietro 29, 35139 Padova, Italy
Paola.golinelli@inwind.it

Paola Golinelli  

Through psychoanalysis, we have come to understand that cinema facilitates the representation of unconscious impressions and fears (Gabbard, 1997) and constitutes an unexpected source for exploring, at greater depths, experiences that are often beyond the intentions of film-makers and financiers. This seems to be the case with Castaway by Robert Zemeckis, which, conceived as a mass-consumption film, ends up revealing the typical anxieties of contemporary man having to come to terms with his most elementary needs and recognise his affective dependence. These anxieties extend from the vision of becoming a homeless urban reject to the modern nightmare of being suddenly reduced to affective homelessness, marooned on an ‘atoll’ of unreachable solitude. This may lead someone to ‘shipwreck’ on the analyst couch and, so, to undertake a long and difficult voyage.

The ever topical setting of the film is a ‘Robinson Crusoe’ (Defoe, 1719) desert island, where the individual comes face-to-face with his limitations. In the eighteenth-century world of Crusoe, the relationship with the inner and external environment, however apparently hostile and unknown, is in the end reassuring, showing the individual's capacity to take possession of the surroundings and become enriched through contact. On the contrary, Chuck Nolan (Tom Hanks), a FedEx manager, faces the paradox of being accustomed to living in an external environment of growing comfort and ease, in contrast to his inner world, where his relationship with his objects lacks continuity and he runs the risk of losing contact with them. In the novel, the island where Crusoe was shipwrecked, although at first deserted and inhospitable, gradually fills up with animals and eventually even a human being, called Friday. This involves the reader in an exciting and enriching fantasy. However, in Castaway, the modern spectator feels a growing anxiety, landing together with Chuck Nolan in a ‘world’ where life is increasingly suspended and stationary, and the only other human being is the corpse of another unfortunate traveller.

responseSet.title: Reviewing editorial peer review: A further step towards developing common editorial standards
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reviewing editorial peer review

A further step towards developing common editorial standards

Maria Ponsi

Maria Ponsi

Via della Chiesa 77, 50125 Firenze, Italy
mariaponsi@dada.it

Maria Ponsi  

Dear Sirs,

David Tuckett's proposal of developing a methodology for evaluating psychoanalytic papers (‘Evaluating psychoanalytic papers: Towards the development of common editorial standards’ (IJP 1998, 79: 431-48)) is grounded in a concern for the insecure foundations of psychoanalysis, which on the one hand ‘has been far from developing a means and methodology to have and to resolve debates by building an informed consensus’ and on the other has shown a ‘tendency to multiply schools and paradigms and to rely on rhetoric and argument by authority’ (p. 431).

A way to counter what has weakened our discipline is to give ‘greater formal attention to the development of a more rigorous culture of enquiry’ (p. 432), which—as regards written contributions—implies establishing a shared methodology of evaluating them.

While illustrating the methodological criteria to be applied in our discipline Tuckett insists on the issue of clarity and rigour as against the issue of ‘truth’ (p. 445): authors should be free to choose their own different ways of communicating what they want and of setting out their arguments, but they should seek to set out their viewpoint in the clearest and most rigorous way: ‘The emphasis is on asking how we know rather than what we know’ (p. 445).

The evaluation of the quality of a paper is strictly editorial: this means that, while examining a paper for publication, the evaluators should leave what the authors say somehow in the background and pay the greatest attention to the way in which the ideas are presented, how comprehensible they are, how well argued and how widely accessible to the various sub-groups of the psychoanalytic community.

The first requirement in practice is that evaluators abstain from selecting papers on grounds of personal agreement and disagreement. It is not the job of an evaluator to reach a personal opinion as to whether they like the argument. What an evaluator needs to be interested in is the argument the author offers and whether readers are provided with enough to understands, to be enlightened by it and to evaluate it (p. 433).

responseSet.documentRefHTML: Smith-di Biasio, A. (2003) On Helene Troisier review of La violence de l'sinterpretation by Piera Aulagnier. International Journal of Psychoanalysis 84:450

responseSet.title: On Helene Troisier review of La violence de l'sinterpretation by Piera Aulagnier
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Helene Troisier review of La violence de l'sinterpretation by Piera Aulagnier

Anne-Marie Smith-di Biasio

Anne-Marie Smith-di Biasio

83 rue de la Convention, Paris 75015, France
amdibiasio@aol.com

Anne-Marie Smith-di Biasio  

Dear Editors,

As someone engaged in crossing the boundaries between British and French psychoanalysis, both as translator and critic, I am surprised by the review of Piera Aulagnier's La Violence de l'interprétation published in IJP October 2002. The premise for the review, by Hélène Troisier of the Paris Psychoanalytical Society, seems to be the translation into English of this seminal work twenty-six years after the original publication. Yet the review heading, while citing the English title, refers only to the French edition and the review itself, while providing a critical discussion of the original text, makes little of the translation, the time-lapse and the various meanings of this transmission between cultures. Once we begin to sketch out the scenario—the editorial and translation initiatives of the New Library of Psychoanalysis, and the identity of the translator, Alan Sheridan, unmentioned here, who translated Lacan's Four fundamental concepts of psychoanalysis in 1977, four years after their appearance in French—not only can we begin to see a landscape in which the history and dissemination of psychoanalysis takes place and is rooted, but we can also ask questions about processes of transmission and translation against this background. Examples of such questions might be: What is being transferred with the translation, where this word is understood as signifying not solely a passage between two national languages, but as enveloping other forms of passage and of impasse such as transference, which are made readable by the model of analytic listening.

Hélène Troisier refers to Aulagnier's Italian origins and her decision to retain the French patronym of her first marriage. This is an interesting point which might lead us to remember Latin origins and read the French words phantasme, délire, énoncé against the background of their Latin roots. They then take on a materiality and plasticity which the English language is at pains to restore. This point runs deeper than a simple linguistic observation. In fact, it seems to encapsulate a fundamental difference between Anglo-American and Continental psychoanalysis whereby the body of the word and all its metaphorical potential, fully exploited in the European tradition runs the risk of invisibility in English. Can the hallucinatory power of phantasme be translated by ‘fantasy’? Should we not at least restore the spelling ‘phantasy’, introduced by Susan Isaacs in 1948 in an attempt to wrest the word from common English usage, to restore its psychical specificity. Does the word ‘delusional’ translate the spatial and temporal dislocation embodied in the word ‘délirant’, ‘falling out of the groove’? Would not ‘wording’ better represent the material emergence into verbal language traced in Aulagnier's paradigm—pictogramme, phantasme, énoncé

responseSet.title: On Irene Cairo's review of Melting the darkness by Warren Poland
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Irene Cairo's review of Melting the darkness by Warren Poland

Irene Cairo

Irene Cairo

5 West 86th Street, Apt. 6A, New York, NY 10024, USA
irecai@cs.com

Irene Cairo  

Dear Sirs,

My own passion for precision impels me to write. In my own review of Warren Poland's book Melting the darkness (Int. J. Psycho-Anal.2002, 83(6), p. 1465) the quote ‘a way where passion and precision are one’ belongs to Yeats, not to Keats, and such quote of course is correctly identified by Poland in his book. How could this have escaped me, and the much sharper eye of the excellent Duncan Martin-Holloway? Perhaps because on my own I quote Keats (in a very different context). At any rate, please let's all stand corrected, precise and passionate.

451

responseSet.title: Hidden faults: Recognizing and resolving therapeutic disjunctions By Steven A Frankel. Madison, CT: Psychosocial Press. 2000. pp. 212.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Hidden faults: Recognizing and resolving therapeutic disjunctions By Steven A Frankel. Madison, CT: Psychosocial Press. 2000. pp. 212.

Michael Sacks

Michael Sacks

Weill Cornell Medical Center, 525 East 68th Street, New York, NY 10021, USA
mhsacks@mail.med.cornell.edu

Michael Sacks  

Dr Frankel begins with a description of a depressed female patient in a stalemated treatment. The patient insists that the analyst does not understand that something is wrong with her children and she rejects his counterinsistence that this view of herself is excessive depressive self-criticism. When one of the children becomes suicidal, the analyst realises that, rather than understanding the patient's mothering as she herself has been seeing it, he has been a cheerleader. Frankel had failed to distinguish neurotic from realistic guilt, and he had failed fully to appreciate that he was in a stalemate. Both errors with the patient seem to be familiar enough. Whereas most analysts might conclude that theory had been misused in the service of countertransference, Frankel focuses on the misleading focus in the current theory of therapeutic impasses or disjunctions that he believes was at the core of his difficulty.

In order to elucidate this problem, Frankel undertakes the ambitious plan to review his work over a hundred randomly chosen three- to six-month clinical segments from ten years of practice. The book can be viewed as the fruits of that research project. It is intended as both the evidence for a new theory of disjunctions and an exposition of that theory. He consistently finds that disjunctions are a failure in ‘interpersonal engagement, and one that forecloses understanding’. More controversial is the point that such failures are not entirely explained by transferences, countertransferences or projective identifications:

It is important to underscore that the psychology of one or both participants in a disjunction may be relatively free from distortions. The existence of a disjunction in that case reflects the fact that a shared understanding between the two has simply not yet been achieved. It may relate to the innocent misunderstanding of cultural and experiential differences (p. 53).

It is this ‘innocent misunderstanding’ that Frankel suggests is at the core of disjunction. It is a ‘hidden fault’ in the fabric of analytic relationships. I suspect Frankel might enlarge this to include all relationships. In numerous case examples, he provides an examination of what was going on with himself and how this might have influenced the disjunctions. These personal issues are openly discussed, and the author provides sufficient material to begin to give a good idea how the disjunction between his world and the world of the patient occurs. The recurrent discovery in each of the presentations is his difficulty in monitoring a tendency to know or understand the patient too easily.

responseSet.title: Un tempo per il dolore: Eros, dolore e colpa: [A time for pain: Eros, pain and guilt] By Tonia Cancrini Preface by Antonino Ferro. Torino: Bollati Boringhieri. 2002. 192 pp.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Un tempo per il dolore: Eros, dolore e colpa

[A time for pain: Eros, pain and guilt] By Tonia Cancrini Preface by Antonino Ferro. Torino: Bollati Boringhieri. 2002. 192 pp.

Giuseppe Squitier

Giuseppe Squitier

Via Brunico 6, I-00136 Roma, Italy
g.squitieri@iol.it

Giuseppe Squitier  

The title refers to a biblical verse4 that expresses an essential aspect of human existence: its evolving over time and its necessary inclusion of a multitude of experiences, in which pain plays a large part. If this need is assumed not simply as ‘fate’ but as history—as the evolving of factual and emotional experiences that the mind can confront and work through—human existence can acquire traits of freedom and richness that liberate it, at least partially, from repetition, compulsion and mortification.

This seems to be the springboard for this book by Tonia Cancrini, a training analyst of the Italian Psychoanalytic Society. As she does not hesitate to tell us a number of times throughout the book, she comes from a philosophical background with much experience in philosophical research. This provenance can be discerned in her sophisticated skills in theoretical analysis (especially in the ninth chapter, devoted to Freud's Civilization and its discontents) and in her scholarly use of literary and philosophical sources (especially classical Greek philosophy, but also such authors as Hegel, Schopenauer and Jaspers). This book is not, however, a theoretical treatise; it is, in actual fact, many things collected together into a unitary, original whole.

Let us see how the author herself defines it in her Introduction:

In this book I have tried to bring others closer to my pain and to the suffering of those I have attempted to understand, communicating in this way the sense of my life and my profession. This is a book of stories, tales and experiences: my life, the lives of people I have been fortunate to get close to and perhaps sometimes to help. You understand others through your own experiences, as Bettleheim (1967) stated so well, ‘and this is why my story and their stories, the stories of those people who entrusted part of their lives to me, are so closely intertwined’(p. 31).

The reader must not, however, imagine that this is an autobiography—not at all; in spite of the fact that the book is crowded with clinical histories (or rather, therapeutic relationships) and also with characters and events from the author's personal life. The narrative style of the text stems naturally from Cancrini's specific way of being. It results from her identity as an analyst having reached a full degree of maturity that coincides with her simply being a person and confers on to her a capacity of expression which is at the same time both free and disciplined. The book reconstructs this path through a composite, but ordered, whole of memories, sensations, experiences, clinical sessions and theoretical observations, and its particular nature is revealed in a most distinctive style of writing, far from usual in psychoanalytical texts.

responseSet.documentRef: Micati, L. (2003) L'empatia psicoanalitica: [Psychoanalytic empathy] By Stefano Bolognini Preface by Alberto Semi. Commentary by Vincenzo Bonaminio. Torino: Bollati Boringhieri. 2002. 224 pp.. International Journal of Psychoanalysis  84:1411-1414

responseSet.title: L'empatia psicoanalitica: [Psychoanalytic empathy] By Stefano Bolognini Preface by Alberto Semi. Commentary by Vincenzo Bonaminio. Torino: Bollati Boringhieri. 2002. 224 pp.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

L'empatia psicoanalitica

[Psychoanalytic empathy] By Stefano Bolognini Preface by Alberto Semi. Commentary by Vincenzo Bonaminio. Torino: Bollati Boringhieri. 2002. 224 pp.

Loredana Micati

Loredana Micati

Via Brunico 6, I-00135 Roma, Italy
brunico6@iol.it

Loredana Micati  

The thorough and knowledgeable research undertaken by Stefano Bolognini for this book displays some of the best characteristics of Italian psychoanalysis. This is a psychoanalysis that has not precociously developed its own strong thinking but rather has worked hard and in depth with the available psychoanalytic theories. This has allowed for an open approach that has proved to be very fertile in the development of ideas.

Even more than interesting and pleasing, this book is testimony to a style of life and thought—to a way of being a psychoanalyst that is composed of passion, dedication and competence, coupled with a well-mannered and self-directed irony. Quite the opposite of a superficial reader, Bolognini is an attentive one, approaching other authors with great respect. Right from the start, in the Introduction, where he reflects on the implicit micro-theories that constitute the psychoanalyst's fundamental theoretical repertoire, he distinguishes between eclecticism—the superficial and furtive appropriation of other models—from a respect for the complexity involved in the articulation or even integration of the theoretical constructions of others.

The historical part of the book is most useful, in that the author carefully reviews the reflections of those who have previously worked with the concept of empathy. In this he reflects on different theoretical and technical points of view, and investigates their implications.

Bolognini starts from the romantic use of the term, laden with projective and fusional elements. Considering the authors who have previously investigated the relationship between Freud and the concept of empathy (Pigman, Shafer, Fine), he reflects on the position of Freud, who, even if he considered empathy to be a useful, indeed, fundamental condition, nevertheless never ceased to warn us of an excessive involvement. Bolognini comments, ‘In reality Freud was always very preoccupied with, and for many years even diffident of, analysts' reactions to their patients, and this fear needed to be extended even to the analyst's “pathein”’.

responseSet.documentRef: Esman, A. H. (2003) Imagination and the meaningful brain. By Arnold H. Modell, Cambridge, MA: MIT Press. 2003. pp 253+xiv pp.. International Journal of Psychoanalysis  84:1656-1658

responseSet.documentRefXML: Esman, A. H. (2003) Imagination and the meaningful brain. By Arnold H. Modell, Cambridge, MA: MIT Press. 2003. pp 253+xiv pp.. International Journal of Psychoanalysis 84:1656-1658
responseSet.documentInfoXML: 2003 84 6 1656-1658 Imagination and the meaningful brain. By Arnold H. Modell, Cambridge, MA: MIT Press. 2003. pp 253+xiv pp. Aaron H. Esman 115 East 86th Street, New York, NY 10028, USA ahesman@earthlink.ne/urlt
responseSet.title: Imagination and the meaningful brain. By Arnold H. Modell, Cambridge, MA: MIT Press. 2003. pp 253+xiv pp.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Imagination and the meaningful brain. By Arnold H. Modell, Cambridge, MA: MIT Press. 2003. pp 253+xiv pp.

Aaron Esman

Aaron Esman

115 East 86th Street, New York, NY 10028, USA
ahesman@earthlink.ne/urlt

Aaron Esman  

Recent developments in such cognate fields as cognitive psychology and neuroscience have presented psychoanalysis with both major challenges and major opportunities. The challenges involve the daunting task of demonstrating its continued relevance to the science of mind, increasingly dominated as the latter is by these ostensibly ‘hard’ sciences, which can, often justifiably, claim a degree of empirical support that psychoanalysis is believed to lack. The opportunities reside in the possibility of developing collaborative and integrative investigative programs with scholars and practitioners in such allied fields, and of formulating hypotheses that can, at least potentially, be tested—from the couch, on the one side, and from the laboratory, on the other.

In this, the latest of several reflective volumes, Modell seeks to respond to these challenges, primarily by asserting for psychoanalysis and for the science of mind in general the continuing validity of the concept of a subjective, affectively rooted unconscious as the vital element in mental life. In doing so he pits himself against, on the one hand, those cognitive psychologists who would restrict any notion of unconscious mentation to the role of procedural memory—essentially what Hartmann spoke of as ‘preconscious automatisms’—and, on the other, those neuroscientists who seek to reduce mental activities to purely neuronal events and thereby appear to abolish entirely the concept of mind as we have known it.

Modell explicitly and decisively refutes the Cartesian mind-body dualism. In his view, stated insistently, ‘mind’ is always and inevitably embodied, and all mental events are in one way or another metaphorically grounded in bodily experience. He is, that is, a committed monist; as he puts it, ‘the unconscious can be nothing other than a neurophysiological process’ (p. xii). At the same time he predicates his argument on the proposition that ‘the unconscious contains potential meaning’ (p. 199).

responseSet.documentRef: Günter, M. (2003) Mutterseelenallein. Kunst, form und psychoanalyse: [All alone. Art, form and psychoanalysis] by Reimut Reiche Frankfurt/M. Basel: Stroemfeld/Nexus. 2001. 200 pp.. International Journal of Psychoanalysis  84:1658-1661

responseSet.title: Mutterseelenallein. Kunst, form und psychoanalyse: [All alone. Art, form and psychoanalysis] by Reimut Reiche Frankfurt/M. Basel: Stroemfeld/Nexus. 2001. 200 pp.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Mutterseelenallein. Kunst, form und psychoanalyse

[All alone. Art, form and psychoanalysis] by Reimut Reiche Frankfurt/M. Basel: Stroemfeld/Nexus. 2001. 200 pp.

Michael Günter

Michael Günter

Osianderstr. 14, D-72072 Tübingen, Germany
michael.guenter@med.uni-tuebingen.de

Michael Günter  

Reiche's book diverges sharply from the mainstream of psychoanalytic art interpretation. He first takes issue with psychopathography—as it was first developed in Freud's Leonardo essay (1910)—which was primarily based on establishing the congruence between the work as a symptom and the author's psychic structure. This method of approach had, in fact, already been criticised by art historians—thoroughly and with some accurate arguments—particularly in the dispute over Freud's Leonardo essay. Next, Reiche criticises the systematic utilisation of the observer's countertransference for psychoanalytic art interpretation: ‘The great attraction of this method for all friends of psychoanalysis is at the same time its Achilles' heel: whatever emotional response I have to the work is placed at the centre and announced as countertransference’ (p. 30). This, he argues, opens the way to wild interpretations and essentially serves to confirm long-established hypotheses.

responseSet.title: On the logic of psychoanalytic theory
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On the logic of psychoanalytic theory

Johann Schülein

Johann Schülein

Augasse 2-6, A-1090 Vienna, Austria
johann.schuelein@wu-wien.ac.at

Johann Schülein  

psychoanalytic theory, epistemology, denotative vs connotative theories, multiparadigmatism, institutionalisation

Psychoanalytic theory shows some specific features and problems. It exists in a number of variations, according to different schools as well as cultural and subcultural conditions, with different understandings even of core concepts. Instead of producing definite knowledge, results remain uncertain. They vary in use and imply a permanent reworking of ideas and conceptions. This is the effect of the kind of theory psychoanalysishas to use. Since psychodynamics are a special kind of heterogeneous, changing, always different, emergent—in a word, autopoietic—reality, psychoanalysis cannot use the methods of a denotative theory (algorithmic reduction leading to strictly defined and formulated calculations) but has to use connotative theories. Connotative theories use open concepts which provide an active and flexible access to autopoietic reality. They are able to cope with the difference between singularities as well as with the distance between general logic and empirical reality. Problems tied to this possibility are structural fuzziness, a dependence on forms of use, multiple paradigms and difficulties in legitimation and balance of theories. This causes problems of institutionalisation. These problems are not a sign of immaturity but the normal way in which connotative theories appear and develop. They can therefore not be eliminated but only be treated in a better way.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Comment la féminité vient aux femmes [How femininity comes to women] By Jacqueline Godfrind-Haber. Paris: Presses Universitaires de France, Collection Építres. Pp. 149. 1 st edn. 2001

Hélène Parat

Hélène Parat

71 bis rue du Cardinal Lemoine, 75005
Paris, France —helene.parat@wanadoo.fr

Hélène Parat  

From the outset the prologue of Comment la féminité vient aux femmes poses what are considered by its author to be the essential questions addressed by her investigation into femininity.

177

In the course of this theoretical study, which is nevertheless centred throughout on the analytic treatment of women by a woman analyst very attentive to the effects of her own countertransference on the transference situation, Jacqueline Godfrind-Haber examines the feminine narcissistic foundations of the oedipal organisation. ‘How is a feminine identity to be conceived of upon which the construction and expression of femininity is based?’ ‘Does the archaic have a gender?’ These are the kinds of questions which give direction to this fundamentally clinical inquiry. Homosexuality, both primary and secondary, is the guiding thread of this book, which includes and extends articles that were published between 1988 and 1996 in the Revue française de psychanalyse and the Revue belge de psychanalyse.

In her research Jacqueline Godfrind-Haber, who is a member of the editorial committee for the Revue belge de psychanalyse and a titular member of the Belgian Psychoanalytic Society, as well as one of the latter's former presidents, successfully illustrates the richness of the constant exchange, both clinical and theoretical, between the different societies which meet at the French-speaking Congress. A point they clearly have in common is the inescapable reference to infantile sexuality, culminating in the oedipal organisation.

In this latest book the author sets out to explore issues such as the archaic, narcissistic vulnerabilities and difficulties in constructing identity, without, however, at any point losing sight of the difference between the sexes and infantile sexuality. This concern was already tangible in her earlier book Les deux courants du transfert (1993), in which she distinguished between the different levels of transference in terms of the relative predominance of narcissistic or neurotic registers. The relations between these two registers are here made more explicit, and Godfrind-Haber avoids the stumbling block of focusing too exclusively on either one or the other. In six dense chapters, she explores the question of feminine identity without neglecting, at any stage, to link up archaic problems to their subsequent oedipal effects.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Adolescencia: Del goce orgànico al hallazgo de objeto [Adolescence: From organic pleasure to the finding of the object] By Susana Estela Quiroga, Buenos Aires: Editorial de la Universidad de Buenos Aires. Pp. 299. 1 st end. 1999.

Ramón Urzúa

Ramón Urzúa

Conceptión, Chile

Ramón Urzúa  

This book is a compilation of papers written during the many years the author, professor of adolescent studies at the University of Buenos Aires, has devoted to research and teaching on

182

this subject. It embraces several stages of her work and, inevitably for a book of collected papers, involves a certain amount of repetition of ideas. However, the overall result is an all-encompassing effort to cover the multiple aspects of the stage of life that goes from childhood to adulthood, elaborating a metapsychological framework (Freudian metapsychology) in its strictest sense.

Adolescence has been a topic of growing interest since the work of Stanley G. Hall. Throughout the twentieth century it was commonplace to say that adolescence constitutes a field for interdisciplinary and multi-professional work. This the author reflects in one of her key concerns: the multi-determination of adolescent symptoms and patterns of behaviour. Thus the book ranges from the initial review chapters centering on biology, to the middle and final parts of the book addressing psychoanalytic theory, in which she presents the intrapsychic elaboration of adolescence together with issues involving the socio-cultural access to adulthood. Clinical material, detailed narratives and texts written by adolescents themselves, such as letters and poems, help to illustrate the author's argument.

One of the key features of this books is its faithful adherence to Freudian ideas: starting with the book's opening comments, Freud is quoted from among his many references about adolescence. These quotations are then compared with our current understanding of adolescent development. Freud's genius is shown in the very real relevance that his views, formulated a century ago, still have for us today. Professor Quiroga has a thorough knowledge of Freudian texts, and she explores in depth complex concepts such as deferred action (Nachträglichkeit), casting considerable light on them as she does so. Her own metapsychological view of adolescence is developed much more closely in line with the original Freudian texts than are those of other contributions, for example, those presented by Peter Blos.

responseSet.title: Weathering the storms: Psychotherapy for psychosis By Murray Jackson London: Karnac Books. Pp. 374. 1st edn. 2001.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Weathering the storms: Psychotherapy for psychosis By Murray Jackson London: Karnac Books. Pp. 374. 1st edn. 2001.

Siobhan Murphy

Siobhan Murphy

Denmark Hill, London, SE5 8AZ, UK

Siobhan Murphy  

Murray Jackson's introduction to this seminal work states that ‘this book is not meant to be a treatise on psychoanalysis, but an exercise in psychoanalytic psychiatry aimed at the integration of psychoanalytic knowledge within clinical psychiatry and psychotherapy’ (p. 6). This statement, which is offered as an apology for not doing full justice to all the analytic writings on psychosis, seems to me to stand alone as a laudable aim. The author's capacity to attempt this task reflects his own background. Born in Australia, he trained in England at the Maudsley Hospital as a psychiatrist. His interest in somatic and psychotic conditions led him to the work of Jung, and he trained as an analytic psychologist. Further clinical work in child and adult psychiatry and his awareness of Melanie Klein's work led to his training as a psychoanalyst with the British Society. While maintaining an analytic practice, he worked at the Maudsley Hospital as a consultant. There he further developed a unit where psychoanalytic principles, in conjunction with biomedical knowledge, were used to treat patients with severe mental illness.

This work was described in his book Unimaginable storms, written with Paul Williams.

The present book, described as a sequel to the above, stems from the author's experience of teaching and supervision in Scandinavia after retirement from the British National Health Service. The psychiatric system he describes in Scandinavia is very different from that in the United Kingdom today. Here, with a few notable exceptions, the use of psychodynamic psychotherapy as part of the treatment of patients with psychotic conditions is very unusual. By contrast, the Scandinavian psychiatric establishment has been widely influenced by a psychotherapeutically-orientated ‘need-adapted’ approach to the treatment of patients with schizophrenia, originally described by Alanen (1997). This model of care was applied to the treatment of all patients with new schizophrenic illnesses in Norway from 1982.

responseSet.title: Discussion of Helmut Hinz's case
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Discussion of Helmut Hinz's case

Ralph Roughton

Ralph Roughton

240 Halah Circle, Atlanta, Georgia 30328
ralphroughton@aol.com

Ralph Roughton  

relational perspective, intersubjectivity, interaction, attunement, shared treatment memory, homosexuality

The author comments on this case report from a relational and intersubjective perspective. He emphasizes the here-and-now; interactive attunement and the emergence and development of the patient's nascent healthy core, more than the interpretation of defense. A case report of clinical material selected and condensed by someone else necessarily lacks many of the subtle, non-verbal clues and shared treatment memory that the author would rely on for his perspective. Thus he approaches this report as a written narrative of manifest interaction and gives his impressions of how he might have understood the material and responded somewhat differently. He concludes that, although he and the treating analyst would have different conceptions of how the pivotal moment emerged, the outcome might not have been very different. Finally, he challenges the common usage of ‘homosexual’ to denote a certain dynamic position in a context that has nothing to do with sexual desire.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Vocabulário contemporâneo de psicanálise

Contemporary dictionary of psychoanalysis by David E. Zimmerman Porto Alegre: Artmed Editora. Pp. 459. 1st edn. 2001.

Roberto Gomes

Roberto Gomes

Rua Annes Dias 154, S.404, Porto Alegre RS, Brazil
Roberto.gomes@terra.com.br

Roberto Gomes  

This book, which is richly illustrated and presents a great wealth of information, and yet remains easy to read and understand, is at the same time current and ambitious. It is current as an academic project aimed at successfully covering the expanding universe of our contemporary psychoanalytic knowledge, and it is ambitious because of the necessary broad spectrum required to do this.

The author responds to this challenge by stating that the best dictionaries of this kind specialise more directly in the work of a given author and his/her respective psychoanalytic school. He believes that, to date, no book has successfully integrated the most important psychoanalytic schools in a way that would offer a synoptic view of the psychoanalytic concepts from pioneering times—Freud and the authors who followed him, the dissidents and those who extended his concepts—up to the contributions that are being made today. His intention is to help clear up a particular prevailing conceptual confusion which is due to the use by different authors of the same terms with distinctly different meanings, or to the fact that one and the same concept receives different labels, all of which could lead to the Babelisations of psychoanalysis.

462

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Fundamentals of a Freudian clinic By Luís Carlos Menezes. São Paulo: Casa do Psicólogo. Pp. 267. 1st edn. 2001.

Renato Mezan

Renato Mezan

Rua Amália de Noronha 198, 05410-010 São Paulo, SP, Brazil

Renato Mezan  

Luís Carlos Menezes is a Brazilian psychoanalyst who studied in France, where he lived for fourteen years and did most of his training at the APF (Association Psychanalytique de France). He returned to Brazil in 1981 and settled in Sao Paulo, where his participation in the scientific life of the Psychoanalytic Society has been remarkable. What makes Menezes's practice and thinking unique is his strongly French orientation, given that the predominant

474

currents in his own Society are marked by English influence, especially by the work of Bion. Naturally, the author does not ignore the different currents that permeate contemporary psychoanalysis, and his texts reflect a continuous debate with them. However, having lived in France in the 1970s, where he did his analysis and supervisions, and also began his clinical practice, Menezes ultimately became a sort of unofficial ambassador of the French orientation to the São Paulo milieu, and even promoted the visit of colleagues from that country to teach courses and perform supervisions in São Paulo: Pierre Fédida, in particular, is one of those who visited us several times thanks to the good offices of Menezes.

This book consists of twenty-three papers, reflecting the various clinical and theoretical interests of the author, and certainly is one of the most important publications in the active editorial landscape of psychoanalysis in our country. The book is divided into four parts, each of them containing several papers: ‘Conceptual references on clinical psychoanalysis’, ‘The diversity of thinking in psychoanalysis’, ‘Hatred and destructiveness in narcissism’ and ‘Other texts’ It is not possible to comment one by one on articles that discuss such a variety of themes; however, it should be observed that an identical conviction permeates each, and gives them their characteristic tone: that of the fecundity of Psychoanalysis to think about what is human and to treat some of its most painful wounds. The clinician's sensitivity comes to the surface in many parts, even and especially when he discusses theoretical issues, since he believes that theory only makes sense if anchored in the reality of transference and working through. Healthy good humour, sometimes tinged by self-irony, modulates his vision of the analytic community — a community Menezes addresses himself to affectionately, but not complacently, in order to avoid the narcissistic and idealising illusions to which we psychoanalysts give in so easily.

[[RunningHead]]

Disappearing persons

Shame and appearance By Benjamin Kilborne. Albany, NY: State University of New York Press. Pp. 192. 1st edn. 2002.

Stephen Sonnenberg

Stephen Sonnenberg

1600 West 38th Street, Suite 403, Austin, Texas 78731, USA

Stephen Sonnenberg  

This volume is actually several books rolled into one. That makes it difficult to read, and difficult to review. The author tells us at one point, ‘In this chapter I have meandered …’ (p. 117), and at another that the creation of the book involved ‘bewildering’ difficulties (page ix). ‘Meandering’ and ‘bewildering’ are terms which might be applied to the experience of reading this work, taken in its entirety. At times I felt as if I were lost in a maze. Yet that perspective alone does not do justice to this text, which is at once a book by a sensitive clinician about a perplexing clinical problem; a book demonstrating the use of applied analysis to examine a psychoanalytic question best viewed through a prism which reaches beyond the examination of an analysand in the consulting room; the work of a trained clinician, anthropologist, historian and literary scholar; and a challenging discourse on philosophy, theology, literature, the visual and dramatic arts, and psychoanalytic history and theory. Later, taking all this into account, I will offer some suggestions about who might benefit from reading this book, and how that would-be reader might go about it.

478

[[RunningHead]]

On “The Castaway Self” by Paola Golinelli

Neil Maizels

Neil Maizels

38 Urquhart Street, Hawthorn, 3122, Australia
neil.maizels@bigpond.com

Neil Maizels  

Dear Sirs,

In response to the film review essay ‘The castaway self …’ by Paola Golinelli (Int. J. Psycho-Anal.2003; 84: 169-72.) I'd like to make two points.

First, the film is not entitled Castaway, but Cast Away. From an analytic viewpoint, that should be a fairly important and complex difference—allowing for many ambiguous shades of meaning in relation to the film's emotional and philosophical impact.

Second, the ending of the film is not ‘unresolved’ or ‘unhappy’. The symbolism of the winged package—linked to ‘angelic’ hope for new paths in life after ‘catastrophic change’—is literally pursued to its end, psycho-geographically, when Chuck No-Land (not Nolan) does take the road towards his new, mysterious, yet destined Muse.

He has a package of love that must be delivered to her, without which, his mere survival on the island where he was cast away is no more than a hill of beans.

His smiling realisation of this means that his previously stultifying (oedipal) crossroad is transformed (through the death of the technologically pseudo-omnipotent Father-Fedex) into the delivery of a regenerated and reciprocated love, which cannot be timed or planned—only received with Grace.

If that is an unhappy lot for us humans then we are indeed more castaway than Cast Away.

Yours faithfully
Neil Maizels

765

responseSet.title: Response
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response

Paola Golinelli

Paola Golinelli

Via San Pietro 29, 35139 Padova, Italy
paola.golinelli@inwind.it

Paola Golinelli  

Dear Sirs

I thank Neil Maizels for his contribution to the discussion on my paper ‘The castaway self’ and on Zemeckis's film Cast Away.

First, as far as I understand the precise definition he underlines, I would certainly like him to go into the complex difference ‘allowing ambiguous shades of meaning’, that he suggests, but does not explain. He suggests I underestimate the different meanings between Castaway and Cast Away, a miscript I apologise for, but perhaps a creative misreading of the film. A film is made of pictures and I was fascinated by Zemeckis's pictures of the aircrash, of the modern shipwrecking, of the atoll and the raft; they made me think of a castaway dimension of the Self, a metaphor of an inner dimension, that sometimes leads people to desperation, sometimes to undertake psychoanalysis, looking for changement.

Second, I see the risk of a potential misreading of symbols and metaphor, a spoiling and forcing of the director's intentions when Neil Maizels writes ‘the winged package—linked to angelic hope’ etc. We probably act like ‘spoilers’, as Green wrote in 1992, any time we disassemble what is already represented in pictures, symbols, poetic metaphors.

765

responseSet.title: Psychoanalysis, history and subjectivity By Roger Kennedy. Hove and New York: Brunner-Routledge. Pp. 189. 1st edn. 2002.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis, history and subjectivity By Roger Kennedy. Hove and New York: Brunner-Routledge. Pp. 189. 1st edn. 2002.

Michael Molnar

Michael Molnar

Freud Museum, 20 Maresfield Gardens, London NW3 5SX
freud@gn.apc.org

Michael Molnar  

In a previous book, The elusive human subject: A psychoanalytical theory of subject relations, Roger Kennedy proposed a reconceptualisation of subjectivity in psychoanalysis. In this new work he builds on that foundation. In the introduction to Psychoanalysis, history and subjectivity he brings the three themes of his title together:

The past in the clinical encounter can be seen as that which is re-enactable between analyst and patient. I have chosen to organize my own story about history in terms of a particular plot, that of differing models of human subjectivity (p. 4).

The dissonance here between ‘plot’ and ‘models of human subjectivity’ promises a tension between the specific and the abstract that must somehow be resolved if the story is to be held together.

In the third chapter, ‘A history of the closure threat to the Cassel Hospital’, Kennedy shows us one way that balance can be achieved. Relating his firsthand experience of the crisis at the Cassel, he succeeds in presenting the wider picture in both historical and psychological terms.

772

responseSet.title: Infant research and adult treatment: Co-constructing interactions By Beatrice Beebe and Frank Lachmann. Hillsdale, NJ: Analytic Press. Pp. xv + 272. 1st edn. 2002.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Infant research and adult treatment: Co-constructing interactions By Beatrice Beebe and Frank Lachmann. Hillsdale, NJ: Analytic Press. Pp. xv + 272. 1st edn. 2002.

Jeremy Nahum

Jeremy Nahum

36 Birch Hill Road, West Newton, MA 02465-2552, USA
jpnahum@massmed.org

Jeremy Nahum  

Many a psychoanalyst has wondered aloud: infancy research is very interesting, but what does it have to do with psychoanalysis, and of what benefit is its study to the practising psychoanalyst? Beebe and Lachmann's book should put this notion by sceptics and doubters to rest.

The relationship between the two fields has indeed been a curious one, although two of the towering figures in developmental research, Louis Sander and Daniel Stern, are both psychoanalysts. Moreover, psychoanalysis has always thought of itself as a ‘developmental’ psychology: indeed, one would be hard pressed to find any psychoanalytic theorist's work that did not have either explicit or implicit developmental assumptions. Why then, should psychoanalysts not be interested in the empirical research of developmentalists, much of whose work deals with issues we have always considered salient? Happily, relations between the two disciplines continue to evolve in a constructive direction.

782

responseSet.title: Commentary on "An example from child analysis"*
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Commentary on "An example from child analysis"*

Johan Norman

Johan Norman

Johan Norman  

I enjoyed reading Jill Miller's paper, ‘An example from child analysis’, throughout which her honesty and sincerity are clearly evident. I would, however, like to highlight some areas where our understanding and style of analysis differ.

Confusion or infantile transference?

In Miller's complex report, Julia, the 10-year-old analysand, comes to her session with a secret. Both the analyst and the reader are told this secret at the end of the session—that a parallel cognitive treatment has now been terminated. This treatment had previously reduced the frequency of analysis from four to three sessions per week, and its subsequent termination arouses new expectations that the frequency of Julia's analysis could be once more increased. However, it also serves to highlight the possibility of the beginning of the end of the sessions. After five years of treatment, the parents are anxious for the analysis to finish. Julia has this in her mind, but she is probably not consciously aware of it on attending the session.

In the waiting room before the session begins, Julia hides under the table, which is unusual behaviour for her. In my view, this is a beautiful example of the uncertainty that is a precondition for the analytical work: there is the analysand's reality, external and psychic, known by neither the analysand nor the analyst when the session begins.

To this point, I think that Miller and I may share the same view, but it is once the session is under way that I think our analytic aims differ. My impression is that Miller's aim is to work with the analysand's conscious awareness in order to get her out of a confusion. My view, however, is that what can be seen and felt as confusion from the vertex of conscious, secondary-process thinking rather can be seen as a mental state coloured by infantile transference that can be worked out and understood as meaningful. It is present, here and now, and has to become emotionally known in the relationship between the analysand and analyst, received and transformed in the analyst's containment, so that this part of the analysand's reality can be integrated in a bearable form in the analysand's mental functioning.

responseSet.title: On ‘Miscarriages of psychoanalytic treatment with suicidal patients’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On ‘Miscarriages of psychoanalytic treatment with suicidal patients’

Mauro Morra

Mauro Morra

Via Biasioli 321, I-16167 Genova, Italy
morrafr@tin.it

Mauro Morra  

Dear Sirs,

I read Dr Gabbard's (2003) keynote paper with extreme interest. I not only appreciated the clarity of the presentation and the author's open-minded approach to the very severe issues concerned, but I also admired his being able to refrain from any moral judgement and from an easy technical condemnation. Instead, Dr Gabbard tries, I would say desperately, to understand what really went on in both the analyst's and the patient's mind, while also showing sympathy for the therapist who was in a very difficult time of his life.

No doubt the projective identification played a paramount importance in the analyst–patient relationship, as described in the paper, and I would like to elaborate a bit further on this point.

Who is the sexually abused child? In my opinion, it is the analyst. The patient is successful in projecting entirely her own experience of the incestuous relationship with her father into him. Through her seduction, she transforms him into the confused, anxious child, full of guilt and worry for his future, dirty and damaged (as we find in our clinical practice with the victims of incest). In some ways she destroys him, as her father previously had destroyed her.

Already, at the first interview, the analyst was struck by the beauty of the lady (‘the most beautiful woman he had ever seen’). When she confessed that she was attracted to him and asked for a different kind of relationship, he ‘clarified that dating was impossible because their professional relationship had already begun, and turning back the clock was not an option’. So his answer seems dictated only by the superego: we can see the frightened child who knows that becoming the lover of his mother is forbidden on pain of a terrible punishment (Oedipus was blinded and exiled). The acting out of the Oedipus complex is confirmed by the fact that the analyst ‘noted that Jenny looked a lot like his mother’: there is probably a lot of idealisation in this statement.

But Jenny insists, acting as a sexually abusive parent who cheats and blackmails. ‘She tried to make Dr N understand that she was an awful person … At one point he said he would do anything he could to keep her from killing herself. Jenny replied that the only thing that would help was if he allowed her to spend a night with him … Dr N refused and explained that sleeping with a patient was unethical.’ Again the superego! The patient understood that Dr N was in a moral conflict, thus her comment: ‘What is more important? My life or your stupid rules?’ In some ways she would be right, if the only obstacle were the fidelity to abstract rules. Jenny was sure that the analyst could not resist much longer, as he was showing an inability to use the psychoanalytical intervention in order to cope with such a serious and difficult problem. The patient probably had this feeling since the time of their first meeting; I presume that this was the reason why she later adamantly refused the hospitalisation (she was not interested in being cured; she was interested in having him sexually).

responseSet.documentInfoXML: 2003 84 5 1379-1382 Analysis of the under-five child. Edited by Robert L. Tyson, New Haven, CT, USA: Yale Univ. Press. 320 pp. Karen R Strupp 4550 Post Oak PI, Suite 300, Houston, TX 77027, USA krstrupp@aol.com
responseSet.title: Analysis of the under-five child. Edited by Robert L. Tyson, New Haven, CT, USA: Yale Univ. Press. 320 pp.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Analysis of the under-five child. Edited by Robert L. Tyson, New Haven, CT, USA: Yale Univ. Press. 320 pp.

Karen Strupp

Karen Strupp

4550 Post Oak PI, Suite 300, Houston, TX 77027, USA
krstrupp@aol.com

Karen Strupp  

This book makes a much-needed contribution to the literature on child analysis. It will inspire and stimulate the work of both the beginner and the most seasoned analyst. In this

1379

volume, Tyson presents rare and lively accounts of pre-latency analyses and discussions of this unique work by the most experienced child analysts. The analysts who present their work, primarily in an ego-psychology tradition, do so with warmth and candor, and the discussants add a thoughtful and elucidating perspective on this analytic work with very young children, who have often been thought unable to engage in a meaningful analytic process. I regret that I could not give due acknowledgement to all the analysts whose contributions make this book such a rich resource.

The children presented here range in age from 2 years 9 months to 4 years 6 months at the time of referral. Several had experienced physical and medical traumas, such as a congenital urinary tract defect, an organically based eye defect, or were members of families in which incidences of sudden infant death syndrome had occurred. One child's mother became very ill early in the child's infancy and died soon thereafter. Two little encopretic girls are presented—a rarity in the analytic literature. Several other children suffer fears that have significantly derailed their development.

These analysts show us the ways in which the analysis of the young child is both similar to adult analysis and unique. We see in these cases the young child's capacity to form an active treatment alliance, although he or she does not seek treatment voluntarily and is often not aware of inner distress. The children make conscious use of their play and their relationships with their analysts to understand their worries, and we see them engage with their analysts to observe the workings of their own minds. Galatzer Levy's 312-year-old patient who was terrified of his own mind observed how difficult it was to talk about his fantasies—he ‘sometimes wished he hadn't thought of them’. The little boy commented that he knew his fear came from inside him because he was aware that his analyst did not threaten him or disapprove when he reported his fantasies. Parent's 4-year-old patient developed an implicit contract with his analyst. When he was not ready to hear an interpretation, he said, ‘Don't tell me’, and he could ask for his analyst's input by asking him to start a story for their play.

responseSet.title: Vapaan assosiaation paluu: [The return of free association] By Marita Torsti Helsinki: Helsinki Univ. Press. 2001 341 pp.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Vapaan assosiaation paluu

[The return of free association] By Marita Torsti Helsinki: Helsinki Univ. Press.2001341 pp.

Barbara Mattsson

Barbara Mattsson

Björneborgsvägen 5, O, FIN-00350 Helsingfors, Finland
barbara.mattsson@pp.inet.fi

Barbara Mattsson  

Torsti is an author who has taken other paths to those of many of her colleagues in her home country, Finland. She covers areas that are both topical and complex. In this book, the author presents groundbreaking theoretical frameworks, presenting an interesting approach to the way in which the child, with his kinaesthetic capacities, creates his first potential space. She develops these ideas further, to describe the beginning of the symbolisation process, and couples together alertness and obstruction in free association.

Torsti has also achieved recognition beyond the borders of Finland. Her scientific work has been published in the Scandinavian Scientific Review, the International Journal of Psychoanalysis and the Psychoanalytic Study of the Child. The present book consists of a number of papers and presentations collected from 1988 to the present.

In this book we learn from her experience of diverse areas within psychotherapy and psychoanalysis. Among other topics, Torsti tackles problems in the treatment of borderline disturbances, psychosomatic disorders and trauma, motherhood and the feminine self. This takes us gradually forward to the central chapter on the beginning of the symbolisation process.

The author is also deeply engaged with questions that impact on the essence of psychoanalysis. In a number of different articles, she returns to the concern that something important to the core of psychoanalysis is on the verge of becoming lost. Here she is following in the footsteps of Andre Green when she proposes that the ego-psychology approach and object-relations theory, with their emphasis on preconscious processes in the transference and linear models of development, tend to dominate the mind of the analyst at the cost of an understanding of the unconscious.

1Translated by Keith Jones.1387

responseSet.documentRefHTML: Kales, E. F. (2003) Body double as body politic: Psychosocial myth and cultural binary in Fatal Attraction. International Journal of Psychoanalysis 84:1631-1637

responseSet.title: Body double as body politic: Psychosocial myth and cultural binary in Fatal Attraction
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Body double as body politic

Psychosocial myth and cultural binary in Fatal Attraction

Emily Kales

Emily Kales

30 Bartlett Avenue, Arlington, MA 02476, USA
efkales@comcast.net

Emily Kales  

When Fatal Attraction appeared in 1987 it was generally dismissed as a formulaic Hollywood horror movie. The narrative is all too predictable (particularly for those familiar with Adrian Lyne's other work): a happily married Manhattan attorney Dan Gallagher (Michael Douglas) engages in a weekend sexual encounter with a single woman named Alex Forrest (Glenn Close) while his family is out of town. The woman refuses to relinquish the relationship and threatens to destroy his domestic life. Neither Lyne nor screenwriter James Deardon are interested in investigating the dynamics of marital infidelity or the motivations driving the husband's behavior. Instead the film retreats to the conventions of the horror-film genre precisely because it is unable to resolve cultural conflicts and sexual anxieties that are split off and ‘projected’ on to the cinema screen. Just as dreams, fairytales and myths require interpretation to yield their underlying psychosocial significance, so too the monstrous alien forces and violent images of the typical ‘slasher’ movie contain unconscious and unresolved material that are never commented upon in any conscious way. In its inability to express or wrestle with ambivalence using insight capacities the popular genre film thus exhibits the ‘blind spot’ that has led to the characterization of the screen itself as ‘something of a symptom’ by film theorist Mayne (1990, p. 41).

In this context, reading the film as cultural myth, manifesting the contemporary collective psyche and socio-political reality of its time, proves a more productive enterprise. Fatal Attraction belongs to a group of Hollywood films that emerged in the postfeminist period of the nineteen eighties and early nineties which might well be called ‘backlash’ films, a popular cultural response to changes in women's economic and political status. Such films as Sea of Love (1990), Basic Instinct (1992), Black Widow (1987), Disclosure (1994) and Working Girl (1988) depict women who either through sexual seduction or professional power seek to dominate and destroy a male protagonist by their drive to possess, devour or annihilate him. Psychoanalytic interpretations of the cinematic convention of the predatory woman stalking a helpless male (Lurie, 1981-2; Modelski, 1988; Gabbard and Gabbard, 1993) have suggested that Hollywood mainstream cinema, for the most part produced and directed by males (although Fatal Attraction was co-produced by a woman, Sherry Lansing), are projections of male anxiety about their ‘castration’ and loss of potency—i.e. loss of exclusive dominance and entitlement in the workplace, the bedroom, the military and other institutional domains.

[[RunningHead]]

Dreams that turn over a page: Paradoxical dreams in psychoanalysis, by Jean-Michel Quinodoz Translated by Philip Slotkin. London: Routledge. Pp. 106. 1st edn. 2002.

Margot Waddell

Margot Waddell

120 Belsize Lane, London NW3 5BA, UK

Margot Waddell  

Jean-Michel Quinodoz is a psychoanalytic writer of unusual modesty, clarity and accessibility. He also has an enviable capacity to keep his eye very strictly on his chosen ball. In his earlier book, The taming of solitude (1999), that ‘ball’ was a specific phenomenon observable towards the ending of an analysis, arising from the working through of separation and object-loss anxieties. Quinodoz describes the phenomenon as being that of ‘buoyancy’ — an unsatisfactory translation of the French ‘portance’, signifying the patient's experience of a kind of pleasurable uplift, a sense of being able to ‘fly with his own wings’. This sensation occurs as a consequence of being able, and enabled, to work through the mourning process and to draw, increasingly, on the internalised containing function of the analyst, rather than depending solely on the external presence.

454

Response

Marcia Cavell

Marcia Cavell

1570 Olympus Avenue, Berkeley, CA 94708, USA
cavell@uclink.berkeley.edu

Marcia Cavell  

Dear Sirs,

Dr Goldberg wonders if the articles in the philosophy series are properly refereed, since Tyler Burge speaks of qualia in his article on perception, whereas Dr Metzinger denies that qualia exist. In philosophy, as in psychoanalysis, there is no one and only right position on any question. The hope is that the arguments in this series are presented sufficiently clearly for the reader to

766

spot where some of the interesting issues may lie. About qualia: people who know this area of philosophy much better than I do tell me that ‘holding there are qualia’ covers a range of views—including ones that do not take qualia as individuals; ‘they’ can be properties or aspects of awareness. Given such a broad understanding, most philosophers (outside a few eliminationists like Dennett, Quine, Churchlands) believe in qualia, though there is dispute about what this belief comes to. I might add that I am not familiar with Dr Metzinger's work, though perhaps I should be.

Respectfully,
MARCIA CAVELL
Editor of the Philosophy series

767

responseSet.title: Commentary 1—On How One Sees Catherine
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Commentary 1—On How One Sees Catherine

Arnold Goldberg

Arnold Goldberg

122 S Michigan Avenue, Suite 1305B, Chicago, IL 60603, USA
docaig@aol.com

Arnold Goldberg  

Since we know that our theories are our eyes and ears, it follows that we are unable to take notice of anything without some preconceived notion, and so we are also unable to be surprised unless we know what to look for. Rudi Vermote is good enough to tell us that he carries with him an attitude offered by Bion, and he later refers frequently to Klein. Each of these bits of information helps us to see that patient somewhat as he sees her. Those of us who equip ourselves with other theoretical visions can therefore be either surprised or dismayed at what Vermote makes of his patient, and so we may struggle to capture the sense of his interventions. The well-known admonition of Bion to approach a patient without memory or desire is readily discounted, inasmuch as that very direction awakens a desire in us. We are never able to be free of what we expect to see and hear.

1422

Jeffrey Prager

Jeffrey Prager

Los Angeles, CA. 90095-1551, USA
jprager@soc.ucla.edu

Jeffrey Prager  

Perhaps inescapably, The Freud encyclopedia: Theory, therapy and culture is an uneven and sometimes odd assemblage of entries, comprising terms, concepts, controversies, biographies and institutional histories that in one way or another are linked to Freud and the history of psychoanalysis. The book is beautifully produced by Routledge and, while one is unlikely to read its six hundred and forty pages from beginning to end, the entries, one by one, are readable and engaging, generally accessible to a wide readership but also of interest to practising analysts as well as to those academics for whom Freud and Freudianism are important points of reference.

1073

responseSet.title: On Perception by Tyler Burge
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Perception by Tyler Burge

Arnold Goldberg

Arnold Goldberg

122 South Michigan Avenue, Suite 1305, Chicago, IL 60603, USA
docaig@aol.com

Arnold Goldberg  

Dear Sirs,

As much as one must applaud the effort of the editors to offer selected articles on philosophy to be published in the IJP, I cannot help but wonder if these are properly refereed submissions and if there is not a certain danger in their being accepted without criticism or comment. As but one example, in the submission on perception by Tyler Burge he speaks of qualia and perceptual anti-individualism. Yet in a book by Metzinger, himself a noted philosopher, it is stated unequivocally that qualia do not exist, as well as insisting on a position quite distinct from Burge in terms of the latter issue. As a non-philosopher I am unable to make any sort of reasonable decision about this issue, and I wonder if more balanced presentations would not be preferable. I do not in any way mean this as a criticism of Tyler Burge, who is an outstanding scholar, but more as a matter of general editorial policy.

responseSet.title: Response to André Haynal
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to André Haynal

Charles Brenner

Charles Brenner

Charles Brenner  

Dr Haynal raises many interesting questions for discussion. Because of space restrictions, I shall limit myself to those that are most directly concerned with the question of the moment:‘Is the structural model still useful?’

From beginning to end Freud adhered to the theory that the mind is best understood as composed of separable agencies, systems or structures. These he called, first, Cs., Pcs. and Ucs. and, later, ego, superego and id. The first of these formulations came to be called the topographic theory (Dr Haynal prefers the name‘topic’) and the second the structural theory. Unfortunately, the two terms are often used to refer to the whole of psychoanalytic theory. In order to avoid ambiguity, I tried to make clear in my presentation that I do not propose to change the whole of psychoanalytic theory. What I suggest is that the theory that the mind is composed of separable structures is no longer a valid theory and that it should be replaced by the formulation outlined in my paper.

Interestingly, Dr Haynal asserts that the so-called structural theory was not a theory of Freud's. He says that it doesn't exist in Freud's writings and is a creation of Hartmann's and Rapaport's. In saying so he seems to be equating the term‘structural theory’ with‘ego-psychology’. Later in his paper he attributes ego-psychology to the United States and contrasts it with psychoanalysis as practiced and understood in England and Europe. Yet Dr Haynal must know that Hartmann was an intimate of Freud and that he practiced analysis in Vienna and later in Paris before ever coming to the United States. The two major texts with the word‘ego’ in their titles are A. Freud's The ego and the mechanisms of defence and Hartmann's The ego and the problem of adaptation. They were published within three years of one another while both authors were still in Vienna. Moreover, A. Freud's professional center, after Vienna, was always England, not the United States.

1101

responseSet.title: Rabindranath Tagore and Freudian thought
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Rabindranath Tagore and Freudian thought

Santanu Biswas

Santanu Biswas

Aelite-824, 3 Bidhan Shishu Sarani, Calcutta 700054, India
santanu6@cal3.vsnl.net.in

Santanu Biswas  

submerged consciousness, futile encounter, resistance, opposition, Chakravarty, Das, meaningless verse, tribute, influence

The paper draws our attention to the Indian poet and philosopher Rabindranath Tagore's notion of psychoanalysis. Focusing on the period between 1926 and his death in 1941, during which Tagore had not only met Freud but had also spoken to several persons on psychoanalysis, the author has unearthed a mass of archival material, primarily in Bengali, and translated most of it into English for the first time, in order to show how Tagore's opinion regarding Freudian thought gradually changed from severe criticism and a near complete rejection to appreciation, especially of its good use in literature and literary criticism. The author has also identified a number of literary works, both prose and verse, from the final years of Tagore's life and tried to document the extent of influence of Freudian thought on their composition. In short, the author explores a significant interdisciplinary area that has not been looked into either in India or abroad.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Disappearing and reviving: Sandor Ferenczi in the history of psychoanalysisAndré E. Haynal. London and New York: Karnac pp. 151

Judit Szekacs

Judit Szekacs

40 New End Square, London NW3 1LS
jszekacs@dircon.co.uk

Judit Szekacs  

The project of ‘rediscovering’ Sandor Ferenczi has been an extremely interesting chapter in the recent history of psychoanalysis. In 1988 the formation of the Sandor Ferenczi Society in Budapest marked the moment of a possible new beginning—a time for working through the trauma that our capacity of psychoanalytical understanding has suffered by enactments of ‘cumulative splitting’ during the formative years of our discipline. This has opened up the way for recalling and reintegrating the image, and basic contributions of, Ferenczi and several other ‘lost children’ of the early analytical world. It has also made certain areas of our ‘forgotten knowledge’ rethinkable. These are concepts regarding the working of body-and-mind; the development and function of reality testing; the nature of individual and social traumatism; mutuality; technique; methodology; and also the dynamics of social development—including the vicissitudes of individual and group relations within the frames of our national and international institutes and societies, to mention just a few.

Over the last decade Ferenczi Societies have been formed in different countries.

794

responseSet.title: The life and behavior of living organism: A general theory. By Elliot Jaques, Westport: Praeger. 2002 302 pp.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The life and behavior of living organism: A general theory. By Elliot Jaques, Westport: Praeger. 2002302 pp.

Larry Hirschhorn

Larry Hirschhorn

Philadelphia, PA, 19103 USA
Lhirschhorn@mail.cfar.com

Larry Hirschhorn  

Psychoanalysis begins with our wishes and fantasies and recounts the drama of how each of us, initially frustrated by reality, comes to recognize its inevitability. As a result, we do not relinquish our desires, but instead, using what resources reality offers us, devise roundabout ways to fulfill our wishes. We learn with difficulty to satisfy our needs, though there is always a margin of need—Lacan calls it desire—which remains unsatisfied. Psychoanalysts, however, are less interested in the dynamics or mechanics of how we go about bending reality to our wishes. This is the working of the adaptive ego, which uses the ‘secondary process’, one of the two principles of mental functioning. When under the sway of the secondary process, thinking takes the form of trial actions. An individual reasons out how a particular action may or may not lead to a desired result. In this sense, the mechanics of secondary thinking are based on the logic of cause and effect, or instrumental thinking, and are the work of consciousness. Psychoanalysis, by contrast, describes the unconscious. This distinction parallels the difference between cognitive-behavioral and psychoanalytic therapies. Working in the former tradition, the therapist helps the patient solve problems by honing her reasoning skills. Working in the latter tradition, the therapist helps the patient loosen the hold of the primary process and the pleasure principle just enough to allow for problem solving in a real world. When that happens, the patient is on her own, because, from a psychoanalytic point of view, reason is inhibited only when the primary process dominates. Analysis restores the person's autonomy by enabling the patient to access her conflict-free reasoning abilities.

In this book, Jaques, begins just where psychoanalysis ends. He wants to understand the mechanics of the secondary process. A fertile and creative management theorist, Jaques began his career as a physician and psychoanalyst. He was analyzed by Melanie Klein and, after World War II, joined the Tavistock Institute of Human Relations as one of the first generation of researchers and practitioners to apply psychoanalytic thinking to organizational and social systems. But as Jaques (1988) recounts, he broke with his peers upon learning, from an engagement with an engineering company, that managerial accountability and the structure of authority, rather than group dynamics, established the basis for good work.

responseSet.title: The social edges of psychoanalysis By Neil J Smelser. Berkeley and Los Angeles, CA: Univ. of California Press. Pp. 263. 1st edn. 1998.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The social edges of psychoanalysis By Neil J Smelser. Berkeley and Los Angeles, CA: Univ. of California Press. Pp. 263. 1st edn. 1998.

David Tuckett

David Tuckett

London WC1 6BT
D.Tuckett@ucl.ac.uk

David Tuckett  

Neil Smelser is a very distinguished sociologist, University Professor at the University of California, a past president of the American Sociological Association, and formerly Director of the Center for Advanced Study in the Behavioral Sciences at Stanford. This book contains an account of his various contacts with psychoanalysis and his attempts at interdisciplinary synthesis. Smelser began his career as an undergraduate at Harvard (1948-52), where, after graduating, and after a visit to Oxford to study economics, he collaborated with Talcott Parsons to develop a sociological understanding of economics. He was a member of that generation of intelligent and thoughtful social scientists of the very first rank (among them Parsons, Clyde Kluckholm, Gardner Lindzey and Robert Bales), who pushed their own discipline forward and took part in a university culture in which psychoanalytic ideas and therapy were ‘the unofficial but dominant currency’, both at social gatherings and in the Harvard Department of Social Relations. When he moved to Berkeley in the 1960s, Smelser trained as a psychoanalyst at the San Francisco Institute. He worked in the UC Berkeley student counselling service and collaborated intellectually with Robert Wallerstein and Erik Erikson. Throughout his career Smelser has been very active in university administration, and he engaged with student radicals in the 1960s and affirmative action in the 1990s—on which some of these papers reflect.

481

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A new European Editor for the IJP

Glen Gabbard and Paul Williams

Paul Williams

Paul Williams  

We are delighted to announce that Dott. Antonino Ferro has been appointed to the post of European Editor of the International Journal of Psychonalysis, to take effect from 1 August 2003. He succeeds Jean-Michel Quinodoz, a tribute to whom follows this announcement. Antonino Ferro is an ideal person to take up the mantle of the European Editorship of the IJP, given his wide involvement in European psychoanalysis and extensive experience in psychoanalytic publishing. Born in Palermo in 1947, Antonino Ferro obtained his Classical School Leaving Certificate followed by a First Class degree in Medicine and Surgery at Palermo University. At Pavia University he specialised in psychiatry, gaining First Class honours, and worked on a national research centre project concerned with the prevention of mental diseases (from 1976 to 1983). Following an early interest in the fields of drug addiction and psycho-geriatrics he became involved in child neuro-psychiatry and this led to his setting up and directing a Centre for Motor and Language Rehabilitation. He subsequently became a lecturer at Pavia University Psychiatric Centre and Milan University Child Psychotherapy Centre, where he occupied the post of Professor of Child Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy. He is currently a Training and Supervising analyst for the SPI, based in Milan, where he conducts analyses of adults, adolescents and children. He has a particular interest in the analysis of severely disturbed patients. Ferro has always been interested in the study of contrasting and competing models in psychoanalysis. This interest has led him to debate and work with colleagues from all parts of the world. Two areas of study in which he has excelled are the concept of the ‘Psychoanalytic Field’ postulated by Baranger in Argentina, and the theories and clinical methods of Wilfred Bion.

Antonino is also an enthusiastic teacher, providing supervision, lectures and seminars for psychoanalytic societies in many countries, particularly on the thought of Bion and Winnicott. He has been Scientific Secretary for the SPI and President of the Milan Centre for Psychoanalysis. His publishing and editorial experience is extensive. He has written a number of books, most of which have been translated into a variety of languages, and many articles for the principal psychoanalytic journals, in particular the IJP. He is on the editorial board of the Revista de Psicoanalisis del'APA—Buenos Aires; the Journal of Melanie Klein and Object Relations—New York; and the Revista de Psiqiatria do Rio Grande do Sul—Porto Alegre. He is Editor of the Italian Annual of the IJP; Editor of the Quaderni di Psicoterapia Infantile published by Borla; he has executive responsibility for the series Frontiers in Groups in Psychoanalysis, also by Borla; he is a member of the EPS Working Party on Clinical Issues and has participated on the IPA Committee ‘On relations between psychoanalysis and contemporary sciences’. He performs a valuable service summarising the most important articles in the Italian Psychoanalytical Review for publication to readers of the Psychoanalytic Quarterly.
