responseSet.title: Verdi's Rigoletto: The dialectic interplay of the psychic positions in seemingly ‘mindless’ violence
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Verdi's Rigoletto

The dialectic interplay of the psychic positions in seemingly ‘mindless’ violence

Moshe Bergstein

Moshe Bergstein

Hakotzer 4, Ramat-Hasharon 47 411, Israel
moshb@post.tau.ac.il

Moshe Bergstein  

Rigoletto, mindless violence, psychic positions, primitive mental states, internal object representations

In this paper the author demonstrates the interplay of the psychic positions (paranoid-schizoid and depressive, alongside more primitive mental states) as they may appear in experiences of overwhelming anxieties relating to fragmentation and disintegration. These are examined in relation to the appearance of what has been described in the literature as ‘mindless’ violence. The vehicle for this demonstration is Verdi's opera Rigoletto, which the author examines using Fonagy and Target's model for the understanding of mindless violence. The opera is ‘read’ as a case study of the protagonist, using the drama as a reflection of Rigoletto's internal object representations. The author attempts to extend Fonagy and Target's model to include primitive mental states, in order to understand ‘mindless’ violence as a result of collapse of the dialectical relationship of the psychic positions, leading to the violent act as a desperate attempt at re-establishing a sense of self. From this perspective, the moment of the violent act may be seen as use of an autistic object and as supplying of autistic shape. The violence is ultimately directed against the already fragmenting self, especially cherished parts of it, however it may enable acknowledgement of repudiated, unmentalised emotions previously experienced as a void.

responseSet.title: Response to Dr Szecsödy
responseSet.authorMast: Elizabeth L. Auchincloss and  Robert Michels
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to Dr Szecsödy

Elizabeth Auchincloss and Robert Michels

Robert Michels

525 E 68th St, New York, NY 10021, USA
elauchin@mail.med.cornell.edu

Robert Michels  

Dear Sirs,

We are grateful to Professor Szecsödy for his thoughtful and detailed response to our paper. We are in full agreement with him that we must constantly monitor the culture of psychoanalytic education for evidence of the cult of the ‘ideal psychoanalytic personality’. It is our continued hope that promoting an academic culture which rewards scholarship, critical enquiry and research rather than only identification with one's idealised elders will contribute to the ongoing task that he so cogently delineates.

responseSet.title: Questions about questions: New views on an old prejudice
responseSet.authorMast: Paulo L.R. Sousa,  Ricardo T. Pinheiro and  Ricardo A. Silva
responseSet.authorCitation: Sousa, P. L., Pinheiro, R. T. & Silva, R. A.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Questions about questions

New views on an old prejudice

Paulo Sousa , Ricardo Pinheiro and Ricardo Silva

Ricardo Silva

Barroso 1202, sala G107 CEP 96010-280– Pelotas, RS – Brazil
sousa@phoenix.ucpel.tche.br

Barroso 1202, sala G107 CEP 96010-280– Pelotas, RS – Brazil
ricardop@terra.com.br

Barroso 1202, sala G107 CEP 96010-280– Pelotas, RS – Brazil
ricardo. as@uol.com.br

Ricardo Silva  

questions, curiosity, speech act, dialogues, technical strategies

The belief that good analysts do not ask, and do not answer, questions is a still-living strange recommendation, transmitted throughout psychoanalytic generations. The authors have extensively reviewed electronic databases and technical texts. They found that interrogative acts are largely omitted from technical considerations, generating a prejudice that does not consider questions as proper psychoanalytic tools, and they discuss possible foundations for this belief. The authors believe that the few authors that have touched on this issue have done so in a non-systematic and frequently incomplete form. Linguistic philosophy presents questions as speech acts: actions that are able to alter the equilibrium of dialogical discourses. This view permits psychoanalysts to understand the potentiality of questions to introduce psychic change because they can, simultaneously, interfere in the self, in the other and in the intersubjective relationship. An internal state of curiosity is described as a component of the mind of the analyst. Based on the argument of the ubiquity of the state of curiosity of the analyst, the authors carried out a set of technical propositions, regarding, on the one hand, when questions are not to be introduced (for instance, in the presence of free associations) and, on the other hand, when they would be useful as expanding factors of the preconscious-conscious system. Considering psychoanalyses as experiences of curiosity, they emphasize that both the internal state of curiosity of the analyst, and the eventual verbal questions put to the patient are potentially positive factors for psychic change. The authors present several clinical vignettes.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

La ferita dello sguardo: Una ricerca psicoanalitica sulla melanconia

The wounded gaze: Psychoanalytic research on melancholia1 by Patrizia Cupelloni. Milan: Franco Angeli. 2002. pp. 188.

Paola Golinelli

Paola Golinelli

Via San Pietro 29, I-35139 Padua, Italy
paola.golinelli@inwind.it

Paola Golinelli  

The authors of this book, edited by Patrizia Cupelloni, are ten psychoanalysts who six years ago founded a research group on melancholia. Readers will sense immediately that they are being introduced to a methodological tool of great refinement and precision and are being invited to share in a deeply rooted passion for Freud's text. The approach to Freud draws on reassessments made by a number of authors, mainly from the French School—among whom Abraham, Torok, Green, Laplanche, Pontalis and Lambotte stand out—together with Winnicott's fundamental line of interpretation.

Within the framework of a shared theoretical–clinical discourse, and independently of the approaches of other schools, each of these ten authors has succeeded in carving out a very personal course of research and analytic style. In detail they examine specific elements relating to melancholy, which they put forward as the central concept of psychoanalytic research in place of ‘depression’, a much-misused term in common and psychiatric language. In so doing, they give back to melancholy that profoundly human dimension that makes it so fascinating. Who does not recognise himself in Dante's ‘Un dì si venne a me melanconia/e disse: io voglio un poco stare teco’ [One day melancholy came to me/ and said: I want to stay awhile with you] (Rime, XXXIV)?

The melancholic person's feeling of emptiness is traced back to the foundations of the mind itself—to that psychic dimension that Patrizia Cupelloni, author of ‘La cripta del silenzio’ [The crypt of silence], calls ‘prehistoric, more than infantile’ (p. 99). The primitiveness that the authors write about is not the primitiveness bound up with the vicissitudes of aggression and its projective reprisals in the relationship with the object described by Kleinian and post-Kleinian writers; the hypothesis that melancholic illness is caused by a traumatic factor converging with an instinctual, constitutional element, for them, points elsewhere. Rather, these authors investigate a pre-representative area of mental functioning, not dissimilar to, although earlier than, that described by Green (1983) as the complex of ‘the dead mother’—that is, the operation of disinvestment that can provoke either the formation of a ‘hole’ in the fabric of object relationships or reliance on a primary method of identification.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The power of the inner judge: Psychodynamic treatment of the severe neuroses. By Leon Wurmser. Northvale, NJ: Jason Aronson. Pp. 356, 2000.

Eric Marcus

Eric Marcus

4 East 89th Street, New York, NY 10128, USA
erm4@columbia.edu

Eric Marcus  

It is an honour to review this important book, which makes a major contribution to the psychoanalytic treatment of very ill psychiatric patients and to ego-psychology.

The book begins with an introduction, which gives an overview and places the book within the context of the history of, and current issues in, the psychoanalytic treatment of very ill psychiatric patients. The next chapter describes Wurmser's views on defence and superego analysis. There are then three chapters with verbatim case material illustrating and elaborating Wurmser's technique, focusing especially on superego pathology. The last two chapters address and illustrate issues of two clinical problems affecting this group of patients: global affects and the problem of the repetition compulsion.

The writing is dense. Wurmser's scholarship is broad and deep. This rich book reads like a seminar with one of our most erudite teachers. It is a pleasure to read if you find such books pleasurable. I find them extremely pleasurable because they satisfy one's need for theory, for clinical examples, for help with technique and for the literary elaborations and allusions that deepen one's understanding, dazzle one's associations and maintain one's excitement.

The case material is wonderful and unusual in its rich detail and in what it shows of the specific, verbatim process. Wurmser is among those brave few who dare to reveal to our collegial scrutiny exactly what they do. His work is incisive, articulate, precise and humane. He is open about his errors as well as his successes, the countertransference as well as the transference. This is case material in relation to which one can have no doubt about what is being done. Because the case material demonstrates new clinical technique with this group of cases, and because it illustrates new clinical theory, the case material is crucial and enlightening as well as interesting.

The major clinical contribution of this book is its focus on superego pathology in very ill psychiatric patients. Wurmser deals methodically and specifically with superego rigidities, attacks, lapses, organisations and enactments. More importantly, he describes and applies a new term, the split superego. Each side of this split is in conflict with the other. Each side may be enacted. Typically enacted and more obvious is the impulse-ridden, aggressive side. More importantly, for Wurmser, is the covert, masochistic and self-punishing side.

responseSet.title: The Pale Criminal: Psychoanalytic Perspectives By Stephen J Costello. London and New York: Karnac Books. Pp. 181. 1st edn. 2002.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Pale Criminal: Psychoanalytic Perspectives By Stephen J Costello. London and New York: Karnac Books. Pp. 181. 1st edn. 2002.

Christopher Cordess

Christopher Cordess

16 Highlever Road, London W10 6PS, UK
c.c.cordess@sheffield.ac.uk

Christopher Cordess  

The ‘pale criminal’ is a term and concept originally taken from the title of a chapter of Nietzsche's philosophical work Thus spake Zarathustra (1961). It was developed by Freud in his short paper ‘Criminals from a sense of guilt’ (part of ‘Some character types met with in psycho-analytic work’), as well as elsewhere, to refer to those criminals whose ‘unconscious guilt’ precedes and—so it is maintained—causes their later crimes. ‘Pale criminals’ are ‘pale’ because they suffer unconscious guilt for an idea, or phantasy, which is traced to an unresolved Oedipus complex; they are in Jung's phrase ‘slain by their own ideas’, and ‘do not feel well in their skin’: they are ‘criminal’ in order to provoke punishment. By seeking and achieving punishment the pale criminal is said to rationalise and purge his/her sense of unconscious guilt. In the words of Stephen Costello, the author of this volume, ‘this is the motive of their misdeeds, after which they (supposedly) become quiet and content’. In brief, their conscience is not too weak but, rather, it is too strong. Freud (1924) later replaced the usage of the term ‘unconscious guilt’, since feelings cannot properly be described as unconscious; he, along with Theodore Reik, substituted the term ‘need for punishment’. Costello contrasts the pale criminal to the ‘rosy criminal’, a term also borrowed from Nietzsche, who is said to possess neither conscious nor unconscious guilt—although he/she may, apparently, feel shame.

469

responseSet.title: Affect intolerance in patient and analyst By Stanley Coen. Northvale, NJ and London. Pp. 290. 1st edn. 2002.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Affect intolerance in patient and analyst By Stanley Coen. Northvale, NJ and London. Pp. 290. 1st edn. 2002.

Richard Almond

Richard Almond

550 Hamilton Avenue, Suite 339, Palo Alto, CA 94301, USA
rjalmond@leland.stanford.edu

Richard Almond  

Stanley Coen writes as I imagine he works as an analyst—personally, directly, engagingly. He is not the distant, omniscient theorist-clinician that we encounter so often in the first century of psychoanalytic writing. Coen is a real person, telling us about his (and our) struggles with tough patients—his feelings, mistakes, consultations, disagreements—all with a tone of good sense and a willingness to reconsider. Perhaps disconcerting to the student who would like authoritative statements, but for therapists and analysts who have ‘been there’, Coen's new volume, Affect intolerance in patient and analyst, has the ring of truth.

Coen's mission is the exploration of how and why the partners in the analytic dyad evade knowing and discussing their feelings. In the spirit of our increasingly democratic psychoanalytic times, he considers the analyst's affects and defences against affects alongside those of the patient. Inevitably, this bilateral focus takes Coen into discussions of countertransference, relational theories and technical issues of self-revelation. He challenges us to discuss these controversial themes openly, without finger pointing and dismissiveness. Acknowledging his origins as a ‘traditional’ analyst—meaning American ego-psychological, given his training at the Columbia Institute in New York City—Coen looks for additional help in other points of view. In the work he describes in this volume, much of it involving patients with serious character difficulties, he finds help in contemporary Kleinians as well as contemporary Freudians and intersubjectivists. More to the point, Coen often takes us with him to meetings and seminars where he presents the cases he talks about in the book. We hear both the comments of specific analytic discussants and Coen's reactions to them. We hear Coen reacting to case presentations by others, noting their ability to tolerate the patient's or their own affects, and to work with feelings in the process. It is this sort of ‘You are there’ quality to Coen's writing that makes it lively.

responseSet.title: The complete correspondence of Sigmund Freud and Karl Abraham 1907-1925 Edited by Ernst Falzeder. London and New York: Karnac Books, 2002. pp. 650.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The complete correspondence of Sigmund Freud and Karl Abraham 1907-1925 Edited by Ernst Falzeder.1 London and New York: Karnac Books, 2002. pp. 650.

Paul Roazen

Paul Roazen

73 Prince St, Cambridge, MA 02139, USA
proazen@comcast.net

Paul Roazen  

This absolutely splendidly edited book replaces the earlier abridged edition of the Freud–Abraham letters (Abraham and Freud, 1965)—one that had many defects associated with the

1Translated by Caroline Schwarzacher with the collaboration of Christine Trollope and Klara Majthényi King. Introduction by André? Haynal and Ernst Falzeder.1075

period in which it appeared. The experienced translator Eric Mosbacher was so offended by the role Abraham's daughter Hilda played in that 1965 book that he refused to have his real name attached, and therefore the pseudonym ‘Bernard Marsh’ appears alongside the name of Hilda Abraham as co-translator. Someday, three more major examples of Freud's published correspondences will also need redoing: those to Lou Andreas-Salomé, Oskar Pfister and Arnold Zweig are at present incomplete. In the meantime it looks as if the letters between Freud and Max Eitington will be the next to appear, although commercial considerations could make translations into English of that very considerable amount of text questionable. It may be that the Freud–Rank correspondence will be out not long after that. Since the ultimate extent of all Freud's letters could well exceed in size the ‘Standard Edition’, future generations should have the benefit of what many of us may not live long enough to savour. Intellectual historians are going to have plenty of work cut out for them. In the case of Jung, for example, his children never even read the letters between their parents—some one thousand of them—on the grounds of not wanting to trespass on their privacy. Something like the same number of letters exists between Freud and his wife, Martha, some of which Ernest Jones selected for use in his official biography; but a full edition will be an event in the history of ideas.

Although Abraham was not as personally dear to Freud as Ferenczi, and these letters therefore lack the fuller intimacy that is to be found in the recent wonderful Freud–Ferenczi volumes (Brabant et al, 1993; Falzeder et al, 1996, 2000), Abraham did succeed in creating the Institute in Berlin, as well as leaving a significant body of writings and also training important analysands. Freud's correspondence with him, therefore, is, for me, a truly engrossing reading experience. The excellent notes by Falzeder, a great expert on Freud's manuscripts and handwriting, leave the field permanently indebted to him. Falzeder played a key part in the Freud–Ferenczi letters, too, but that project was such an extensive one as to have involved many others as well, under the leadership of André Haynal in Geneva.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis international: A guide to psychoanalysis throughout the world Vol. 1: Europe; Vol 2: America, Asia, Australia, further European countries, Edited by Peter Kutter. Stuttgart: Frommann-Holzboog. Vol. 1, 1992. 327 pp. Vol.2, 1995. 378 pp.

Robert Tyson

Robert Tyson

5174 Chelsea St, La Jolla, CA 92037, USA
Robert@tysonz.com

Robert Tyson  

In these two important volumes Kutter has tried to do for psychoanalysis what Wells (1921) did for history. Although the scope of Wells's subject matter was admittedly much greater, paradoxically his task was easier because a large part of history's evidence is in the public domain as compared with that of psychoanalysis. Thus Wells could write his history by himself, while Kutter had to resort to separate authors for each chapter. Consequently, while the value of the individual contributions depends on each author's training, experience and perspective, the overall significance of the total work is greater than the sum of its parts. Because history continues to unfold, it is as true for Wells's objectives as it is for Kutter's that their results represent a snapshot or cross-section of the subject seen in a perspective of history to that point.

In his preface to Volume 1, Etchegoyen states: ‘The main schools and directions in which the discipline created by Sigmund Freud has evolved are given an enlightening critical exposition’ (p. vii). This seemingly innocuous statement is of much greater import than perhaps even Etchegoyen himself recognized. Despite the multiple authoring, or perhaps because of it, this work constitutes the best available published English-language antidote to psychoanalytic parochialism and Balkanization of theory and practice. The vital core elements of psychoanalysis continue to survive challenges and confrontations around the world, with episodic catastrophic exceptions such as took place during the Nazi regime. There are other more in-depth studies of psychoanalytic thought and of particular psychoanalytic organizations (e.g. Jones, 1953, 1955; Hale, 1971; Gay, 1988; Kurzweil, 1989; Brecht et al., 1993; Wallerstein, 1998; Kirsner, 2000; and the work of the International Association for the History of Psychoanalysis, Paris). However, there is nothing else that successfully depicts the global discipline coupled with views of the ambient intellectual, social and political climate within which individual psychoanalytic societies exist.

responseSet.title: The observing ego as voyeur
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The observing ego as voyeur

Lawrence Josephs

Lawrence Josephs

Garden City, Long Island, NY 11530, USA
josephs@adelphi.edu

Lawrence Josephs  

observing ego, self-reflection, seductive superego, primal scene, denial, moral masochism

A resistance to self-observation and self-reflection is discussed in which there is a perversion of the observing ego. The observing ego has been unconsciously recruited in the service of enacting an unconscious fantasy: the fantasy of being an excited observer of a primal scene who is punished for making forbidden observations. This voyeuristic observing ego is pathologically enmeshed in a love triangle with the patient's seductive superego (i.e. identification with the desired but unfaithful parent) and with the patient's punitive superego (i.e. identification with the rivalrous parent). This unconscious scenario is played out in the clinical situation as the patient unreflectively cycles through phases of denial (i.e. self-seduction) and moral masochism (i.e. self-betrayal). A case study illustrates how humor may be employed to free the observing ego from being enthralled by a perverse superego. Humor may unconsciously enable a rebellious attitude toward the omnipotent sadism of a perversely oppressive superego and thus enable the observing ego to break free from its pathological enmeshment.

responseSet.title: A contribution to the development of a psychoanalytical methodology for research into language: A systematic study of narration as an expression of sexuality
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A contribution to the development of a psychoanalytical methodology for research into language

A systematic study of narration as an expression of sexuality

David Maldavsky

David Maldavsky

Calle Republica Arabe Siria 3319, Piso 5B, 1425 Buenos Aires, Argentina
dmaldavsky@elsitio.net

David Maldavsky  

language of eroticism, preconscious, systematic research, narration, method of research, deductive method in psychoanalysis, David Liberman algorithm, scene analysis, meta-psychology and narration, narrative sequences, discourse analysis

Systematic research on language has a solid starting point in Freudian hypotheses on sexuality. This theory is the semantic ground for categorising narrations and providing a basis for the research method. The author places his research in the frame of the systematisation of the Freudian theory of substitute formations in the preconscious. He affirms that these formations are influenced in a particular way by each variant of sexuality. The author proposes an inventory (in Freudian terms) of those variants of sexuality, and he affirms that they can be detected in the discourse. Five universal scenes having the status of a canon, with specific features for each of the ways in which sexuality is manifested, make up narrations. The author describes the features of each narration and provides some examples. He also examines problems related to the use of the method (the coexistence of different variants of sexuality in a single fragment of a narration and the successive steps in the use of the method). The author briefly considers some other problems: the analysis of defences, the study of words and theoretical research. Finally, he examines the validity and reliability of the method.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Le traumatique, répétition et élaboration [Trauma, repetition and elaboration] By Anna Potamianou. Paris: Dunod, Collection Psychismes. Pp. 164. 1st edn. 2001.

Danielle Kaswin-Bonnefond

Danielle Kaswin-Bonnefond

10 rue Saint Benoit, 72000 Le Mans, France
dkb@wanadoo.fr

Danielle Kaswin-Bonnefond  

Anna Potamianou's concept of trauma is a dynamic one. Present at the origin of all psychic activity, trauma can re-emerge at any moment in the treatment, but whatever clinical forms it may take, Potamianou regards its destructive manifestations as expressions of a vital process with an inherent potential for reorganisation.

Potamianou draws together theories of psychic functioning, with its genesis and dysfunctions, as much from Freud as from contemporary psychoanalytic thought. Her two points of reference are constituted by the hallucinatory fulfilment of desire, that allows access to the registers of the pleasure–unpleasure principle, and by the invasive and structuring traumatic lineage and its tonalities.

1Translated by SophieLeighton, MA (Oxon), MA (Sussex).465

responseSet.title: Psychoanalysis, science and masculinity By Karl Figlio. London: Whurr. Pp. 236. 1st edn. 2000.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Psychoanalysis, science and masculinity By Karl Figlio. London: Whurr. Pp. 236. 1st edn. 2000.

Robert Hinshelwood

Robert Hinshelwood

Colchester, Essex, UK
bob@hinsh.freeserve.co.ul

Robert Hinshelwood  

This book claims that the nature of science lies in the eternal human quest for the origins of things. It is not just the application of neutral human rationality; there is a deep-rooted motivation to journey to the beginnings of everything. The book argues, too, that in trying to understand the motivation for that quest we need psychoanalysis: we need to know the unconscious roots of our restless searching. In a word, we have to view the vibrant and heady achievements of science in conjunction with the infantile phantasies of the primal scene. A quest in search of anything is, at root, the quest for the origins of life—and that means mother's body.

Figlio further claims that such a penetration is fraught with all sorts of emotionally significant, sensitive and terrifying associations. Not least is the phallic quality of penetration. He describes phallicism as the violating penetration of an object that has had all its moral and emotional significance stripped from it. Phallic masculinity is, however, but a defence—a denial of the importance of father as procreator with mother; and a squaring up to father's power. The distorting, defensive and maladaptive nature of phallicism, as found in the primitive unconscious, creeps into the attitudes of scientists, and the public's expectations of them. Science then becomes embroiled in problematic attitudes, which in turn provoke a reaction. Hence, Figlio concludes, this is ‘why such an obviously reassuring and beneficial activity [science] inflames such passionate rebuke’ (p. 211). Science has the image of a penetrative, corrupting violation of nature; and psychoanalysis can say where that image comes from.

The moment of discovery in the primal scene is a confrontation for the infant. His own narcissism is assaulted. His assumption that he exclusively occupies the maternal body is banished by the new presence of father. It is the end of narcissism. As Freud described in the Wolf Man case, the infant attempts to avoid this end and will insert himself into the couple, forming in his own mind identifications with either member of the couple. In the Wolf Man's case the infant became stuck in a neurotic identification with mother's female passivity; but in many other cases, the identification is with the penetrating male. It is this latter identification that appears to have been picked up in the modern period by scientific enquiry and enquirers.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Book Notices

CAHN, Raymond (2002). La fin du divan? [The end of the couch?] Paris: Editions Odile Jacob. Pp. 258.

The author shows how, in the present socio-cultural context, and given the frequency of narcissistic pathologies, the psychoanalytic face-to-face situation has become, in its own right, another way of doing analysis in which the role of countertransference as an internal setting proves to be a decisive factor in accommodating and transforming the patient's productions.

CHABERT, Catherine (2003). Féminin mélancholique [Melancholic femininity]. Paris: Presses Universitaires de France. Pp. 184.

This little book, with a provocative title, brings together several articles in which Chabert turns her attention to the ‘hollow dimension’ of the feminine which shelters the child, the infantile and passivity, constituting the point of chrystallisation of bisexuality and identifications in both sexes on the condition that one frees oneself from the fetters of melancholy.

DANON BOILEAU, Laurent (2002). Des enfants sans langage [Children without language]. Paris: Editions Odile Jacob. Pp. 284.

The author asks the question: how it is that, while most children can speak at the age of 2 or 3, there are some who are unable to speak? Why speak and what do we want from speaking to each other? To these questions he brings the theoretical and clinical responses of a psychoanalyst who is also a teacher of linguistics.

DE MIJOLLA, Alain (2003). Freud: Fragments d'une histoire [Freud: Fragments of a history]. Paris: Presses Universitaires de France. Pp. 379.

The man Freud still remains to be understood. The author explores and draws on paths other than those taken by monumental biographies in search of less well-known aspects of Freud's life such as the way he is represented in the cinema, his analytic practice or his taste for research—the taste for an ever-elusive truth.

DUPARC, François (Ed.) (2002). Fenêtres sur I'inconscient [Windows on to the unconscious]. Paris: Delachaux & Niestlé. Pp. 230.

responseSet.title: Commentary on "An example from child analysis"*
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Commentary on "An example from child analysis"*

Florence Guignard

Florence Guignard

Florence Guignard  

Jill Miller's paper about Julia's analytic treatment is both very moving, in the pathology linked to early and repetitive somato-psychic traumas endured by the child, and extremely rich in technical situations for the psychoanalyst to think about.

To begin with the traumatic illness, one has to figure out baby Julia's terrifying panic attacks, being trapped between her internal sensation of unbearable pressure, and the many brutal intrusions of emergency hospitalisation and surgery, coming from outside. Besides, the doctors had prepared her parents for a fatal outcome. How could Julia have developed any safe feeling of self-existence?

Miller describes Julia on their first meeting as a 5-year-old girl who was ‘in a state of panic, at times hysterical and inconsolable, could not be left alone and was having nightmares’. Obviously, her parents' excellent capacities to support Julia had by now been overwhelmed by her state of terrible excitement and anguish, which was impossible to contain properly. Needless to say, had she not had early, intensive and prolonged psychoanalytical care, Julia's future would have been a severe mental retardation, or maybe sheer madness.

Miller outlines how she attempted to help Julia to detect her defensive use of regression to a state of confusion when confronted with frustration and helplessness. She certainly worked hard to help the child differentiate between the violent sensorial, pathological sensations coming out of her damaged brain and the violent emotions and feelings, especially rage, aroused in the transference situation.

She goes on to describe how she succeeded in becoming for Julia an external object of containment and projective identification, in a way that I would relate to Bion's concept of ‘maternal capacity of reverie’ (1962). She ‘loaned’ her own drives’ organisation and ego-superego structure to Julia's disorganised, or maybe non-organised, traumatised Self to help a better intrication of life and death instincts into sexual drives and, subsequently, into ego drives (Guignard, 1997).

[[RunningHead]]

En el camino de la simbolización: Producción del sujeto psíquico

[On the path to symbolization: Production of the psychic subject] By Myrta Casas De Pereda Buenos Aires: Paidos1999353 pp.

Silvia Cantis

Silvia Cantis

Orense 85 3 A, E-28020 Madrid, Spain
scantis@jazzfree.com

Silvia Cantis  

This is a rich, intelligent and original text that offers the reader a striking profusion of new thinking and reflection, and a recontextualizing of concepts, and in which a number of interrelated issues are developed over the course of its three hundred plus pages.

The author, a true enquiring psychoanalyst, communicates her ideas and discoveries of the last few years with enthusiasm and scientific rigour. The book, published in 1999, comprises a series of twenty-one articles written between 1988 and 1997. Many of these works have already been presented at different forums or published in various journals, and the author has updated and revised all of them for this book.

I was struck by Myrta Casas's readiness to allow herself to draw on the ideas of other thinkers, whose rich contributions she links with classical psychoanalytic concepts. With exceptional permeability, she introduces concepts and ideas from other fields that enrich her work and lead her to the challenge of seeking links with ideas originating in other disciplines. That she succeeds in this fruitful task without losing coherency between theory and clinical practice can be seen through the various clinical vignettes of psychoanalysis with children and adults presented in the different chapters of the book.

2Translated by Elaine D. Bolton.1391

responseSet.title: Terrorism and war: Unconscious dynamics of political violence Edited by Coline Covington, Paul Williams, Jean Arundale and Jean Knox. London: Karnac. 2002. 435 pp.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Terrorism and war: Unconscious dynamics of political violence Edited by Coline Covington, Paul Williams, Jean Arundale and Jean Knox. London: Karnac. 2002. 435 pp.

Anton Obholzer

Anton Obholzer

Tavistock Centre, 120 Belsize Lane, London, NW3 5BA, UK
Anton_Obholzer@tavi-port.nhs.uk

Anton Obholzer  

This book is well timed; that is its great strength but perhaps also its minor failing. It is well timed because, with the ‘War on Terrorism’ being the replacement for the Cold War, it is topical both when viewed against the Twin Towers, Afghanistan and Iraq and also when considered in relation to what is to follow over the next decade or decades of the twenty-first century. The minor failing is, in my view, the inclusion of personal accounts and photographs of the Twin Towers attack. The risk of including such specific material is of making the book American-centric, pivoted on that specific incident and presenting a direct reaction to it. It therefore also runs the risk of causing the book to become prematurely dated when it, in fact, addresses issues that will be of major relevance for a long time. For this reason this book should be in print to the widest possible audience for a long time to come. It would also be a great pity and a disservice to society if it were to become or remain a script confined only to the small psychoanalytically and psychotherapeutically informed subset of society. At least some key chapters should be read by opinion formers and politicians worldwide, though to say that is perhaps to go a bit too far, since, as stated in this book, there is no evidence that every politician's having a psychoanalysis would alter political behaviour. Sadly, and notably, it thus follows, too, that reading a clear exposition of the dynamics of terrorism is unlikely to alter the political-cum-governmental situation much. Yet surely, and in spite of this realisation, we must persist at working at understanding the dynamics of political violence, terrorism and war, and this book goes a long way towards doing that.

The book is divided into four sections: ‘Terrorism’, ‘Hatred, enmity and revenge’, ‘Why war’ and ‘The aftermath of war’. Each section has an Introduction by one of the four joint co-editors of the book.

1645

responseSet.title: Perception
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Perception11

Tyler Burge

Tyler Burge

Los Angeles, CA 90024, USA
burge@ucla.edu

Tyler Burge  

anti-individualism, concept, epistemic externalism, epistemic warrant, information, modular, ontogenetic subjectivism, perceptual constancy, representation, sensation, sense-data, sense-perceptual system, sensory system.

The article is an overview of some central philosophical problems associated with perception. It discusses what distinguishes perception from other sensory capacities and from conception. It discusses anti-individualism, a view according to which the nature of a perceptual state is dependent not just causally but for its identity or ‘essence’ on relations to a normal environment in which systems containing that state were formed. It discusses different views about epistemic warrant. By emphasising the deep ways in which human and animal perceptual systems, especially visual systems, are similar, it criticises a dominant view of the last century, in both philosophy and large parts of psychology according to which a range of sophisticated supplementary abilities have to be learned before a child can perceive objective features of the physical world.

[[RunningHead]]

Book notices

AKHTAR S (2003). New clinical realms: Pushing the envelope of theory and technique. Northvale, NJ: Jason Aronson. Pp. 283.

This collection consists of a wide-ranging set of suggestive essays on a diverse set of clinical and diagnostic topics, including the psychic use of inanimate objects across the life span and in psychopathologies, the role of animals in psychic development and clinical syndromes, writer's block, mentoring, the role of poetry in addressing mental pain and the clinician's use of theory.

BARANES JJ (2003). Les balafrés du divan [Those disfigured by the couch]. Foreword by Jean Cournut. Paris: PUF. Pp. 267.

The ‘vatercomplex’ of the early days of psychoanalysis has been replaced by patients whose suffering in the area of identification is dominated by the defensive mechanisms of denial and splitting. The author suggests that we reconsider our concepts of symbolisation by using as our starting point a clinical approach.

BOKANOWSKIT, GUIGNARD F (Eds) (2002). La relation mère-fille: Entrepartage et clivage [The mother-daughter relationship: Between sharing and splitting]. Paris: InPress. Pp. 118.

What is specific in the mother–daughter relationship? What part does it play in the development of femininity and motherhood, in mourning processes and in transgenerational transmission?

BOTELLA C (Ed.) (2002). Penser les limites: Écrits en I'honneur d'André Green [Thoughts on borderlines. Essays in honour of André Green]. Neuchatel: Delachaux et Niestlé. Pp. 660.

A group of psychoanalytic personalities from all over the world and eminent scholars from other disciplines have contributed to this book. Its running theme, ‘borderlines’, has been selected because it represents one of the main objects of André Green's research and because it is considered to be one of the key problems of our times.

BRITZMAN DP (2003). After-education.Anna Freud, Melanie Klein, and psychoanalytic histories of learning. Albany, NY: State Univ. of New York Press. Pp. 214.

This book explores an important area often neglected in contemporary psychoanalytic literature: how practises of teaching and learning in the field of psychoanalysis and education emerge from what the author (Professor of Education, Social and Political Thought, and Women's Studies), describes in her first chapter as ‘difficult knowledge’. In particular, she focuses on the relevance of controversies in child psychoanalysis (especially between the theories of Melanie Klein and Anna Freud) to the wider field of education.

responseSet.title: Experience—What is it?
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Experience—What is it?

H. Erlich

H. Erlich

42 Midbar Sinai St, Jerusalem IL-97 805, Israel
Shmuel.Erlich@huji.ac.il

H. Erlich  

experience, subjectivity, intersubjectivity, object relations, experiential modalities

The author takes a renewed look at the constitutive aspects of experience, looking at it as process rather than contents. Recently more psychoanalytic voices are discernible that argue for the complexity and multi-leveled nature of inner experience. Yet the predominant and preeminent psychoanalytic voice has traditionally emphasized the linearity and single-factored nature of experience and all that is based on it: development, object relations, psychopathology, and treatment. The author offers an understanding of experience as stemming from the operation of two contiguous, ongoing modalities of processing internal and external input, and reflecting two polarities of the subject-object experience: of separateness and instrumentality, and of oneness and ongoing being. Such a conceptual reframing of experience harbors multiple implications for understanding subjectivity and inter-subjectivity, inter-relatedness as well as single-person psychology, and the all-important role of an experiential ‘goodness-of-fit’ in the analytic situation and elsewhere.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Magnitude of trauma and personality change

Klaus Fink

Klaus Fink

Herrenkellergasse 22, 89073 Ulm, Germany
klaus.fink@medizin.uni-ulm.de

Klaus Fink  

trauma, concentration camp, Nazi persecution, depression, time, space

In this paper the author postulates that, in post-traumatic personality structures caused by overwhelming traumatic experiences, pre-traumatic personality features and childhood experiences are of little or no relevance. Sixty-four survivors of Nazi concentration camps are examined, their concentration camp experiences detailed and pre-persecution histories and post-persecution psychopathology studied. The significance of a concentration camp experience is analytically discussed and evaluated. This study shows that 5 2 cases (81.2%) of the 64 survivors of concentration camps presented an almost identical depressive personality structure irrespective of their pre-persecution life history. The 64 survivors of concentration camps are psychologically compared to 78 cases of people who, in view of the menacing circumstances, decided to emigrate and in this way were spared from becoming victims of the Nazi ‘final solution’ Finally, the author discusses the value of psychoanalytical treatment.

responseSet.title: Imre Hermann's Freudian theory of attachment
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Imre Hermann's Freudian theory of attachment

Tomas Geyskens

Tomas Geyskens

Schapenstraat 128, B-3000 Leuven, Belgium
Tomas.Geyskens@hiw.kuleuven.ac.be

Tomas Geyskens  

Imre Hermann, attachment theory, infantile sexuality, clinging, instincts, John Bowlby

In this paper the author addresses some problems concerning the relation between attachment theory and psychoanalysis and sketches the outline of a Freudian theory of attachment, based on the ideas of the Hungarian psychoanalyst lmre Hermann. First, he elucidates the fundamental divergence behind the insults and misunderstandings that have dominated the debate between attachment theory and Freudian psychoanalysis: they differ radically in their conceptions of temporality and of the relation between psychopathology and human nature. Second, the author presents Hermann's work on ‘Clinging—Going-in-search’ (1976) as a theoretical model in which the findings of attachment theory can be integrated in a theory of psychopathology that is radically dimensional and that preserves the methodology of Freudian psychoanalysis. In the third part of the paper, the author discusses the question of whether Hermann's ‘clinging instinct’ is a primary instinct in the Freudian sense, and whether it is an interesting alternative for Freud's ‘death instinct’, as Hermann claims.

responseSet.title: Clinical notes on a case of transvestism in a child
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Clinical notes on a case of transvestism in a child

Luisa Busch de Ahumada

Luisa Busch de Ahumada

Av. Las Heras 3898, 3 “H” 1425 Buenos Aires, Argentina
jahumada@elsitio.net

Luisa Busch de Ahumada  

transvestism, perversion, autism, oral trauma, black hole, primal scene

This clinical presentation describes the therapeutic process of a 5-year-old male child presenting as a main symptom quasi-delusional feminine enactments starting at an early age, which persisted for most of his five-year treatment. This symptom was understood in terms of an attempt at restitution, itself the result of being confronted with an autistic structure stemming from a series of traumatic incidents during the oral phase: his mother's pregnancy abortion, depression and subsequent three-month absence at the end of his first year of life. The clinical material, drawings included, illustrates the interplay of oral, anal and phallic levels, with enactment predominant in sessions. The oral traumatic situation initially led to anal-manic play, then to quasi-delusional female personifications in sessions, later surfacing as an annihilating ‘black hole’. All the above issued into a broad enacted phallic-genital unfolding that dramatised an oral-genital primal scene, in the course of which he managed to structure his male identity. Near the end of the analytic process the analysand reworked the ‘stages’ of the link to the analyst. To end, based on the clinical material, the respective participation of the early and the late Oedipus complex is examined.

responseSet.title: Response to Dr Sanders
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to Dr Sanders

Chris Mawson

Chris Mawson

79 Burkes Road, Beaconsfield, HP9 1EE, UK
c.m@psychoanalysis.org.uk

Chris Mawson  

Dear Sirs,

Thank you for sending me Dr Sanders's letter in which he states that he thought I had made a serious mistake at the outset and proceeded from that basis to write an unfair and irrelevant review.

When I wrote, mid-way through the review (not at the beginning), that the aim of the first chapter was to demonstrate the clarification of confusion using the psychoanalytical method, I was referring to something written by the author in his preface, and not (as Dr Sanders wrote) the passage on p. 5 which mentions what he proposes to describe in the chapter. In the preface the author writes: ‘Chapter one combines a prologue, giving a brief survey of the ground to be covered, with—as the psychoanalytical process is one of clarifying confusion—an account and discussion of a consultation in which this was unmistakable although not accessible to further work’ (p. xii). I think that this is a statement of an aim.

Even if I had made the error which the author mistakenly attributed to me, I don't see how this would render everything else in the review irrelevant.

I accept the author's criticism that I omitted to discuss the way in which dreams are treated in his work.

1624

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Transition to young adulthood

A prospective study

Nathan Szajnberg and Henry Massie

Henry Massie

1720 Lake St, San Francisco, CA 94121, USA
nmoshe@earthlink.net

3036 Regent St., Berkeley, CA 94705, USA
Henrymassie@AOL.com

Henry Massie  

young adulthood, memory, identity, intimacy, prospective

In this paper, the authors report on the transition to young adulthood (18-25) in the highest functioning 18 individuals in our cohort (GAF '90) from a prospective longitudinal study of 76 lives followed from birth. These 18 individuals provide as clear a view as possible into the inner lives of people least distorted by psychopathology. This gives us a more crystalline photograph of psychic structure: the precipitant of family, society and psyche itself. Using a standard DSM IV criterion, Global Assessment Functioning (GAF), provides phenomenological homogeneity, but leaves us with heterogeneity of inner life. The 18 individuals had relatively rare drug or alcohol use and less sexual partners. Vocational functioning exceeded intimacy. Four had tumultuous pathways into adulthood. Nine were crossover cases, coming from sub-optimal mothering, with significant variation in gender distribution and intrapsychic structure. The paper discusses factors facilitating and inhibiting development and the nature of memories.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Adult Attachment Interview and psychoanalytic outcome studies

Siri Gullestad

Siri Gullestad

PO Box 1094 Blindern, N-0317 Oslo, Norway
s.e.gullestad@psyckologi.uio.no

Siri Gullestad  

Adult Attachment Interview, outcome studies, discourse analysis, attachment patterns, relational strategies, therapeutic change

During the last two decades, the Adult Attachment Interview (AAI) has attracted growing interest from psychoanalysts concerned with empirical research. The paper discusses the application of Crittenden's Dynamic-Maturational AAI method for assessing the outcome of psychoanalysis. The aim is to demonstrate, through a case presentation, how therapeutic change can be expressed in the AAI. The pre- and post-treatment interviews of one patient, having completed a four-times-a-week psychoanalysis, are presented. It is demonstrated that the detailed discourse analysis of the AAI, based on transcribed tape-recorded interviews, focuses subtle formal elements of language and speech reflecting dominant patterns of affect regulation and object relating. The AAI text analysis provides possibility for coding procedural memory as conveyed by the handling of the relationship to the interviewer, incorporating the dynamic relationship between researcher and subject and thus complying with a methodological prerequisite regarded by many psychoanalysts as necessary for capturing data that are relevant to psychoanalysis. On this background, the method emerges as promising for psychoanalytic outcome studies.

responseSet.title: On the countertransference of the patient: Transformations of the psychoanalyst's ‘theoretical self’ and their possible articulations with the vicissitudes of the analytic relationship
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On the countertransference of the patient

Transformations of the psychoanalyst's ‘theoretical self’ and their possible articulations with the vicissitudes of the analytic relationship

Cristiano Rocchi

Cristiano Rocchi

Via Scipione Ammirato 38, I-50136 Firenze, Italy
psico@inwind.it

Cristiano Rocchi  

countertransference, precipitates of theoretical self, theoretical self, transference, tropisms

In this paper the author examines one of the many levels of the analyst/analysand relationship: the possible interaction between the analyst's mental routes in relation to theories (also meant, but not only, as internal objects) and the vicissitudes of the psychoanalytic relationships with his patients. The author assumes that an important variable affecting the transformation of certain therapeutic relationships is the change that takes place in the relationship between the analyst and that part of his internal world where his theories find their place. He names this part of his internal world ‘theoretical self’, and ‘precipitates of the analyst's theoretical self’ those complex formations, akin to more or less cohesive conglomerates, that are formed by his relationship to theories and to psychoanalytic institutions, and by the various, ‘personal’ in ternalised objects. The psychoanalyst will relate to these precipitates in a variety of ways, and he will make use of them mostly at an unconscious level in his analytical work; and his patient is likely to ‘react’ to them, almost chemically. The author also offers some working indications: the psychoanalyst, despite his knowledge of some aspects of his own countertransference, is in fact lacking in knowledge, unless he constantly does some extra work focusing on how his mental position (i.e. the relationship with the precipitates of his theoretical self) may intervene in any of the therapeutic relationships that he establishes—not necessarily in the same way in each of them. The author also illustrates his reflections and conceptualisations by reporting dreams and excerpts from sessions taken from three psychoanalytic treatments in the course of several years.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Preconscious defence analysis, memory and structural change

John Ross

John Ross

50 East 78th Street, Suite 1C, New York, NY 10021, USA
LMLRoss@aol.com

John Ross  

intersubjective, intrasubjective, consciousness, preconscious, disavowal, repression, declarative, procedural, structural change, memory

Beginning with the ways in which the use of the couch lends ‘depth to the surface’ (Erikson, 1954), I explore the topography of the inter-and intra subjective psychoanalytic situation and process. I suggest that defences are not by definition unconscious but rather can be observed operating at conscious and preconscious levels, particularly under these conditions. A focus on preconscious disavowal provides a window on what has become unconscious repression. As a result of eliciting and then verbalising the operation of such defences with regard to anxieties in the here-and-now transference, declarative memories of increasingly specific childhood fantasies and events begin to hold sway over unmanageable procedural remnants from the analysand's past. With this may even come the possibility of neuronal regeneration, the more generalisable enhancement of declarative and symbolic functions and the sense of identity with which these are associated. Herein may lie one enduring therapeutic effect of the ‘talking cure’—putting feelings into words—as one among a variety of psychotherapeutic modalities.

responseSet.title: Race, colour and the processes of racialization: New perspectives from group analysis, psychoanalysis and sociology. By Farhad Dalal, Hove, New York: Brunner-Routledge. 2002. 251 pp.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Race, colour and the processes of racialization: New perspectives from group analysis, psychoanalysis and sociology. By Farhad Dalal, Hove, New York: Brunner-Routledge. 2002. 251 pp.

M. Davids

M. Davids

4 Primrose Gardens, London NW3 4TJ, UK
fakhry@bigfoot.com

M. Davids  

This complex book addresses an important topic for psychoanalysis in the twenty-first century. In an increasingly globalised world, how do we come to construe human beings different from ourselves in racialised categories which, in turn, justifies seeing them, and treating them, as sub-human? While such issues may at one time have been judged as beyond the clinical domain of psychoanalysis, this is increasingly being questioned. The Western metropolis—the psychoanalytic heartland—has become increasingly multicultural and multiracial, and patients from minority ethnic groups sometimes feel that their racial or cultural identity is overlooked, presumably for defensive reasons on the part of both patient and analyst (Hamer, 2002). At the very least this reflects a difficulty in engaging with the reality of racial or cultural difference, and the appearance of a work that boldly addresses this problem is thus timely.

The question that underpins Dalal's enquiry is this: if the category of race is indeed an empty one, how does it come to present itself so persuasively as a suitable container for hatred of the other? The thesis of the book is that the processes of racialisation of the other are complex and pervasive; they originate in the external world of power relations and are instituted in, and perpetuated by, racialised thinking in the mind.

1648

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Prima della parola: L'ascolto psicoanalitico del non detto attraverso le forme dell'arte

Before words: psychoanalytic listening to the unsaid through art1 by Antonio Di Benedetto. Milan: Franco Angeli. 2001. pp. 223.

Antonino Ferro

Antonino Ferro

via Cardano 77, I-27100 Pavia, Italy
hmdfe@tin.it

Antonino Ferro  

This beautiful book won its author, Antonio Di Benedetto, the 2001 Gradiva Prize, conferred in the delightful village of Lavarone in the Italian Alps. Freud used to love spending his summer holidays there with his family, in the Hotel du Lac, where he wrote ‘Delusions and dreams in Jensen's Gradiva’. In the very same hotel the Gradiva Prize is today awarded to the best Italian book on psychoanalysis of the year.

The author develops two main concepts. The first is that psychoanalysis should apply art to itself, rather than apply itself to art and lay art on the couch. Psychoanalysis should learn to use art as an inspiration for thought and creativity in its clinical work and theory, following in the footsteps of Freud, who much admired the work of poets and noted, precisely in his essay on William Jensen's Gradiva, how poetic intuition often ‘anticipates’ psychoanalysis. In his Preface, Di Benedetto is already proposing this inversion, the key to the whole book: to pass from a ‘psychoanalysis of art’ to a ‘psychoanalysis by art’, to a science inspired by art, reappraising the poetical roots of psychoanalysis. After all, for every one of us the formation of the alpha element—pictogram, audiogram, coenaestheticgram, olfactogram or whatever it might be—is indeed poetry of the mind. The specificity of the formation of the alpha element makes it infinitely more subjective or poetic than any primal phantasy, which is instead more or less the same in different minds.

The second concept concerns the use and enhancement of ‘sound’—the information value of verbal musicality in psychoanalysis. In clinical practice it is the visual that has always been privileged (in the analyst's reveries, in dreams, in figurative transformations and in representations). It is not by chance that Bion refers more frequently to the visual alpha elements, while acoustic, olfactory and coenaesthetic elements remain little more than implicit.

The author guides the analyst through the first underlying theme towards an attitude that is not limited merely to decodifying—that is, interpreting—the other, but one which encourages him to be—courageously (and perhaps with hubris, passionately)—original and creative in the session with his patient, albeit paying the penalty in doubt and uncertainty that results from a use of subjectivity that is out of conscious awareness.

responseSet.title: Response to Emanuel Berman
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to Emanuel Berman

Joseph Aguayo

Joseph Aguayo

11849 West Olympic Blvd., Suite 202, Los Angeles, California 90064
jaguayophd@earthlink.net

Joseph Aguayo  

Dear Sirs,

It is a pleasure to respond to Emanuel Berman's painstaking read of my ‘Klein-Winnicott’ article. Starting with the easier issue first: Berman corrects a factual error I made (Int. J. Psycho-Anal.83: 1144, fn. 11, last line)

446

in the chronological detail of Winnicott's attempts to include his paper on ‘Transitional objects’ in a publication associated with Klein's contributions. The chance for Winnicott to include what would become his seminal paper was for the 1952 IJPA Festschrift celebrating Klein's seventieth birthday and not the New directions in psychoanalysis volume published in 1955. Fair enough.

In terms of more substantive matters, namely Berman's interpretive disagreements with some of the other points of my paper, I think some of his points are based on a misreading of the paper's main argument. Berman writes that I exaggerated the importance of the unpublished 1937 Klein manuscript ‘Notes on baby.’ I think most archival researchers are aware that the discovery of any unpublished manuscript usually raises the issue of how it meaningfully fits into the published corpus of any major analyst (i.e. is it congruent or at odds with the received portrait we have of the author's work?). My point about ‘Notes on baby’ was fairly straightforward: while Klein neither developed nor published a theory of how environmental influence was mediated, ‘Notes on baby’ demonstrated her sensitivity to the environment, a factor which must be added to other frequent references made by Klein regarding the importance of the environment (Spillius, 1994). Put differently: in terms of the secondary literature, especially writers who follow Winnicott's view that Klein was ‘temperamentally incapable’ of factoring in the role of the environment (Int. J. Psycho-Anal.83: 1136, fn. 4), I simply offered ‘Notes on baby’ as a counter to a long-standing canard that I think is based on an uncritical acceptance of Winnicott's (1962) evaluation of Klein. If there weren't such a widespread misconception that Klein never paid any attention to the role of the environment, such a correction wouldn't be necessary. This reason (among others) makes ‘Notes on baby’ worthy of research consideration.

responseSet.title: On Joseph Aguayo ‘Reassessing the clinical affinity between Melanie Klein and D. W. Winnicott’
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On Joseph Aguayo ‘Reassessing the clinical affinity between Melanie Klein and D. W. Winnicott’

Emanuel Berman

Emanuel Berman

Haifa 31905, Israel
emanuel@psy.haifa.ac.il

Emanuel Berman  

Dear Sirs,

I was intrigued by Joseph Aguayo's paper, ‘Reassessing the clinical affinity between Melanie Klein and D.W. Winnicott’ (Int. J. Psycho-Anal.2002, 83: 1133-52). I share the author's view that the period of close collaboration between the two has not been sufficiently studied, and he raises several interesting points. However, the paper's uncritical view of Klein and its tendency to denigrate Winnicott produce some biased formulations.

When discussing the ‘Transitional objects’ paper, Aguayo states that Winnicott ‘deliberated about whether to include this paper in a commemorative volume for Klein, New directions in psycho-analysis (Klein, Heimann and Money-Kyrle, eds, 1952 [sic]), ultimately missing the publication deadline’ (p. 1144), and says this is detailed by ‘Grosskurth (1986, pp. 397-402)’. I was unable to locate this story in Grosskurth's biography. She states, however:‘Winnicott took his paper to the 1951 editorial meeting at which the selection was to be made for [Klein's] seventieth-birthday issue of the [International] Journal. Klein wanted him to revise the paper so that it more clearly incorporated her ideas. He refused; and with the manuscript under his arm, he sadly left the room’ (1986, p. 398). I do not know the source of the New directions story (actually, that book appeared in 1955, two years after the publication of ‘Transitional objects’), but in any case bringing it up while disregarding Grosskurth's account is misleading.

Aguayo exaggerates, I think, the importance of the unpublished ‘Notes on baby’. It is surely an interesting document, and he deserves gratitude for bringing it to our attention. But Klein's ability to observe in private notes the impact of the parents on the infant in a particular family does not in any way undo the neglect of this same impact in her theorising, and in her published case studies, as Aguayo mentions.

445

responseSet.title: Diagnosing a male hysteric: Don Juan-type
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Diagnosing a male hysteric

Don Juan-type

Trevor Lubbe

Trevor Lubbe

36 Cartsens Street, Tamboerskloof, Cape Town 8001, South Africa
tlubbe@yahoo.com

Trevor Lubbe  

male hysteria, gendering of hysteria, Don Juanism, compulsive sexuality, primal scene, triangulation of separation dynamics

The aim of this paper is to provide a clinical account of a male hysteric, Don Juan-type, taken from the early stages of treatment. The patient presented with a relationship problem but there soon emerged a form of compulsive sexuality or hypersexuality in his love relations that became a central feature of the clinical picture. This hypersexuality expressed itself in a compulsive need to stage, or to stage-manage, interpersonalscenaria of a sexual or sexualised nature. These scenaria, which were repeated in different variations and with different personnel, are seen by the author as a dramatisation of the primal scene with the patient taking up the position of the oedipal father. Explanations for the disappearance of male hysteria are given, including a new theory which claims that an imbalance in psychoanalytic theory itself led to the feminisation of hysteria. This critique allows certain forms of hypersexuality in men to be promoted as a form of hysteria, the most common example being Don Juanism—a form of compulsive sexuality that encompasses normative, conversion and character features. The paper also examines the male hysteric's developmental agenda. What the patient's compulsive sexual tableaux exposed was that he had never faced a separation that was not a triangular experience. This meant that his separations were experienced as two developmental agonies telescoped into one—separation (pre-oedipal) and exclusion (early oedipal). This combination, the author suggests, is so frightening in a particular group of men as to explain the choice of hysteria as opposed to some other choice of neurosis.

responseSet.title: Agonie, clivage et symbolisation [Agony, splitting and symbolisation] By René Roussillon Paris: Presses Universitaires de France. Pp. 258. 1st edn. 1999.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Agonie, clivage et symbolisation [Agony, splitting and symbolisation] By René Roussillon Paris: Presses Universitaires de France. Pp. 258. 1st edn. 1999.

Jean-François Rabain

Jean-François Rabain

5 bis rue Le Tasse, Paris 75116, France
j-f.rabain@wanadoo.fr

Jean-François Rabain  

In this book René Roussillon continues the process that he started in Paradoxes et situations limites de la psychanalyse (Paradoxes and borderline states in psychoanalysis), a remarkable book published by in 1991. Roussillon elaborates and explores in more detail the many clinical and theoretical developments presented there—for example, with a set of brilliant reflections on Winnicott's work.

In the course of various clinical studies Roussillon puts forward ‘a model for understanding the narcissistic sufferings and forms of defence (splittings, somatic bindings, perverse masochism, fetishism etc.) that the psyche sets up in order to suppress the catastrophic return of the agonistic experiences that underlie them’ (p. 4). This book also proposes ‘a model for the splitting that operates in these extreme situations of subjectivity, leading on to technical questions about the treatment of these pathologies’ (p. 4). Finally, on the basis of a consideration of the symbolising function of primary objects—a function that is weak in these cases—a model of primary symbolisation is put forward that completes the traditional concept of symbolisation.

The book therefore presents a unitary model of the process at work in the different forms of narcissistic pathology; this is an alternative and complementary one to Freud's model of neurotic suffering. The model proposed here is based on the hypothesis of an organisation defending itself against the effects of split-off primary trauma and the threat that this trauma continues to pose to the psychic organisation through the repetition compulsion.

In Freud's well-known model of neurosis, the present-day conflict resonates with a historic one connected with infantile sexuality that at the time it was only possible to repress.

1Translated by Sophie Leighton, MA (Oxon), MA (Sussex).192

responseSet.title: Immigration and identity: Turmoil, treatment, and transformation. by Salman Akthar Northvale, NJ: Jason Aronson Inc. pp. 220
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Immigration and identity: Turmoil, treatment, and transformation. by Salman Akthar Northvale, NJ: Jason Aronson Inc. pp. 220

Anna Ornstein

Anna Ornstein

60 Longwood Avenue, Brookline, MA 02446, USA
a2727b@aol.com

Anna Ornstein  

An ocean away past voices

Beckoning with promises of rickshaws, bindis and saris.

Part of my soul inside unknown.

I yearn to learn more,

To be brimming, spilling over

With brown friends who look like me.

Words like chapatti and Om.

Waiting to be found and claimed.

Past Voices by Tara Ramchandani.

This poem by the 16-year-old daughter of an American-Indian immigrant expresses one of the book's major theses—the yearning for and the mourning of the loss of the familiar smells, sounds and language of the old country, a yearning that may extend to the next generation. These twin processes of yearning and mourning, the author tells us, have to be accompanied by a profound change in identity if adjustment to the new country is to be successful.

This is a comprehensive and scholarly book. The data supporting the author's conclusions were gathered from a wide variety of sources—personal recollections and experiences as well as interviews conducted with fellow immigrants from many parts of the world. Although psychoanalytic theory is used most extensively to explain the psychological processes involved, the author also relies on anthropology, sociology and fiction to give depth and breadth to the issues related to immigration. The strategically placed poems throughout the text enhance the book's readability and bring an aesthetic dimension to it.

The organisation of the book alerts the reader to its broadly based conceptualisation. Part I discusses the psychosocial variables associated with immigration; Part II has a chapter not directly related to immigration, describing the development, phenomenology and clinical relevance of identity. The treatment of ‘transformation of identity’ is of the same length as is the introductory statement on identity. The reader may wish for a shorter introduction and a more detailed analysis of the transformation of identity, since this is what is supposed to constitute the heart of the book. Part III takes up the problems involved in cross-cultural psychotherapy and provides a general discussion of the immigrant community and the psychology of the immigrant as psychotherapist.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

La pensée clinique

Clinical thinkingAndré Green. Paris: Odile Jacob, 2002. pp. 359.

Dominique Bourdin

Dominique Bourdin

21 rue Guynemer, F-93700 Drancy, France
d.bourdin@technologist.com

Dominique Bourdin  

Green's thesis is that there is a form of clinical thinking that stimulates patients to remember while fulfilling all the requirements of rigour that pertain to any other form of thinking. Following his Introduction, specially written for this book, in which he defines and characterises the concept of clinical thinking and its requirements, Green draws together various articles that illustrate how this thinking can be implemented.

The term ‘clinical thinking’ contains a creative paradox that acknowledges the workings of a genuine form of thinking in psychoanalytic clinical practice which most closely resembles the analysand's experience and is inseparable from the treatment experience.

1Translated by Sophie Leighton, MA (Oxon), MA (Sussex).1079

responseSet.title: On: Analysis of transference: A North American perspective
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

On: Analysis of transference

A North American perspective

Edmund DeLaCour

Edmund DeLaCour

16 Center Street, #405, Northampton, MA 01060, USA
ndelacour@comcast.net

Edmund DeLaCour  

Dear Sirs,

There is much to appreciate and much controversial ground covered in Henry Smith's (2003) probing, complex and clinically rich depiction of North American trends in the

1624

analysis of transference. In his survey he concentrates especially on the work of four contemporary analysts, beginning with Merton Gill. My objection to his paper is that I believe he fundamentally misconstrues Gill's views and then criticizes him on the basis of that misunderstanding. In doing so he ends up aligning Gill with a point of view about the analyst's objectivity which Gill was actually influential in undermining. Gill's criticism of analytic objectivity helped to pave the way for the more interactive conception of the psychoanalytic process that now characterizes the North American scene. For a historically oriented paper suggesting lines of influence from theorists to current practice, this is an error worth correcting.

Smith begins accurately enough: ‘Gill advocated a particular approach to the transference, focusing on the “plausibility” of the patient's perception of the analyst, which he linked to the analyst's actual behavior …’ (p. 1024). But Smith goes on to conclude erroneously that ‘Gill advocates looking for what is plausible in the patient's transference, which implies that the analyst can judge which of the patient's perceptions are plausible and which are not; in this sense [Gill] relies on the analyst's relative objectivity with respect to the patient’ (p. 1026). He contrasts this unfavorably with Schwaber, who ‘on the other hand, would argue that transference is a perception, not a distortion’ (p. 1026).

This is the opposite of Gill's views on the possibility of analytic objectivity, on the analyst's technical stance, and on transference as distortion. Gill's position in these regards can be seen in two related propositions he developed in his monograph on the analysis of transference: first, that the analyst's claims of objectivity in any given clinical moment is untenable; and, second, that there is inevitably a plausible source in the actual therapeutic interaction for the patient's experience of the relationship. The technical principle that follows from these propositions is that the analyst's enquiry into transference material should be directed toward identifying what the patient is responding to in the therapeutic interaction, leaving it to the patient to make a connection to the past, a connection which, coming from the patient, will have the added benefit of being more convincing. The recommendation Gill makes is to assume the plausibility of the patient's perceptions, not to judge their plausibility. The latter stance would return the analyst to what Gill had argued was a discredited presumption of analytic objectivity and, additionally, could be used by the analyst countertransferentially to defend against the emotional immediacy of the therapeutic interaction. Regarding transference as distortion, Gill believed that such a notion implied that the analyst had a clear and unobstructed view on the truth; in other words, was not a participant in a relationship.

responseSet.title: Response to Dr DeLaCour
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Response to Dr DeLaCour

Henry Smith

Henry Smith

17 Hammond St, Cambridge, MA 02138, USA
henryfsmith@cs.com

Henry Smith  

Dear Sirs,

I want to thank Edmund DeLaCour for giving me the opportunity to clarify what may be ambiguous both about my position and my depiction of Merton Gill's position. Gill and I had a number of conversations over the years about the very issues Dr DeLaCour raises, and I do not wish to misrepresent his point of view.

Gill was a man who so loved to argue that, in a real sense, it seems unfair that we should now be arguing about his views in his absence, although I am sure he would wish no less. As I suggested in a memorial volume on his legacy (Smith, 2000), he argued not only with others, but constantly with himself, changing his perspective when he was persuaded to a better way. In 1954, for example, he was a firm believer in the objectivity of the analyst's position (Gill, 1954). He later said he disagreed with everything he wrote in that paper, as he adopted and promoted a perspectivist and constructivist point of view, arguing strongly that there is a continuous interaction in the analytic work, that the analyst is influencing the patient at all times, and that these influences on the patient's transference must be pursued and understood ‘relentlessly’, as Dr DeLaCour points out. It was, in fact, on the matter of this very relentlessness that his collaborator and friend Irwin Hoffman began to diverge from Gill, suggesting that there is a dialectical movement in the work between interpretive and non-interpretive interventions and that not everything need be put into words.

As Gill himself later acknowledged, he was a man who went ‘to extremes’ (1997, p. 216), and he often had to backtrack from those extremes when others persuaded him they were excessive or that his own clinical work belied them. He was ready to admit as much, as when he acknowledged to Roy Schafer that his 1982 monograph was too single-mindedly focused on the here and now interpretation of the transference.

It is this latter work (Gill, 1982) to which I am referring in my article, when I speak of Gill's reliance on the analyst's ‘relative objectivity’, a phrase to which Dr DeLaCour objects.

responseSet.title: Aux origines féminitiés de la sexualite: [On the female origins of sexuality] by Jacques André Paris: Presses Univ. France. 1995. 161 pp.
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Aux origines féminitiés de la sexualite

[On the female origins of sexuality] by Jacques André Paris: Presses Univ. France. 1995. 161 pp.

Monique Cournut-Janin

Monique Cournut-Janin

4 rue du Vertbois, F-75003, Paris, France
cournut@wanadoo.fr

Monique Cournut-Janin  

As its title would suggest, this book is the fruit of theoretical and clinical research into human psychosexuality. Beyond homosexuality, it is heterosexuality that is said to pose a problem … ‘It is our hypothesis’, André immediately explains, ‘that the kernel of love has some deep affinity with femininity, that femininity is the essence of otherness, or more precisely, of the (loving) openness to this otherness’ (pp. 9-10).

Through a study and comparison of the various Freudian texts on female sexuality, the author affirms Freud's ultimate retreat from a model of femininity that is directly present in his investigations before being eradicated in favour of a masculine model of sexuality that is then applied to both sexes.

In the first chapter, the author discusses ‘Dora’, with a view to the other Freudian approaches to femininity; he observes that ‘there is nothing lacking in the clinical material on Dora that Freud presents as evidence of a repressed primary femininity’ (p. 13) (for example, Dora explores the inside of her purse with her finger). ‘How are we to interpret the fact’, André asks, ‘that nothing (or very little) remains of the hypothesis derived from the treatment in the articles that Freud explicitly devotes to female sexuality?’ (p. 14). With Dora, if Mr K is the current seducer, he merely returns the young girl to the paternal seducer. ‘The danger with which Dora is confronted is not Mr K in the present-day situation but the internal attack of the drives of which he is the arbitrary trigger mechanism’ (p. 15).

Freud had related this ‘excessively economic factor’ for the little girl's elaborative capacities to ‘the early appearance of real genital sensations … whether spontaneous or provoked by seduction and masturbation’. The author's theoretical reference points are those of the French school, in which the object is inconceivable without the drive: the psyche is conceived as a locus for the elaboration and symbolisation of excitations originating from both the external and the internal world. Laplanche's hypothesis of the ‘generalised seduction’ is a further important reference-point in this work.
